wordpress.name: Al_Raglin
wordpress.contentText: Al “Rags” Raglin Class of 2004 - Denver Al Raglin was a respected and admired figure in Colorado’s officiating and community circles. Born in Atlanta, Georgia, he graduated from Booker T. Washington High School in 1950. He went on to study at Metro State College of Denver before beginning a 25-year career in the United States Air Force. Stationed at Lowry Air Force Base and later serving with the 9th Aerospace Wing at the Air Force Academy Financial Center in Colorado Springs, Al retired from military service in 1976. A Dedicated Career in Officiating Al began umpiring in the 1960s and continued until his retirement in 1997. He was widely known for officiating A and B playoff games throughout the 60s and 70s and earned assignments in state playoffs across all classifications. He spent years officiating in major collegiate conferences including the Big Eight and the Western Athletic Conference (WAC), and was active in the Northern Colorado Division Playoffs into the mid-1980s. A Life of Service Beyond the Diamond Off the field, Al gave back tirelessly to his community. He worked with Denver Public Schools’ after-school sports programs, led anger management classes, and volunteered with church groups focused on supporting young children and middle schoolers. Al Raglin’s legacy lives on in Colorado’s officiating history and in the countless lives he touched through his quiet leadership, fairness, and service. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Arny_Karraker
wordpress.contentText: Arny Karraker Class of 2008 - Cortez Born in Holyoke, Colorado in 1954, Arny Karraker graduated from Montezuma-Cortez High School in 1972 and went on to attend Western State College in Gunnison the same year. He and his wife Patti have lived in Cortez, Colorado for many years, where Arny became a respected leader in the local baseball community. A Career of Dedication Behind the Plate Arny’s umpiring career spanned more than 25 years and included countless District and Regional playoff assignments. In 2008, he was honored with a selection to officiate the Colorado State Championship Game. His reputation as a skilled and reliable official extended across the Southwest, with additional assignments in New Mexico, Utah, and Arizona. Arny also contributed at the national level, working Regional and State Championship Games for the American Amateur Baseball Congress (AABC), including high-profile events like the Connie Mack World Series in Farmington, New Mexico. Leadership and Local Impact Since the formation of the local umpiring association in Cortez, Arny served as Area Director — a role he humbly accepted, joking that he took it on because “no one else had the time.” But those who know him recognize his dedication as genuine leadership. Arny had been actively involved in local baseball since the mid-1980s, building trust and camaraderie within the community. A Dream Deferred, Not Forgotten Arny once expressed a wish to become the oldest active umpire in Colorado. Though health challenges eventually required him to step away from active officiating, his impact on the field — and in the lives of fellow umpires and players — remains enduring. Arny Karraker exemplifies what it means to serve the game with heart, humility, and lasting commitment. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Art Wollenweber
wordpress.contentText: Art Wollenweber Class of 1991 Art Wollenweber built an outstanding career at Sheridan High School, earning recognition as one of Colorado’s most accomplished high school coaches. In 1987 he was named the Colorado High School Coaches Association Coach of the Year, capping an impressive run of competitive success and program leadership. Throughout his tenure, Wollenweber earned milestone awards for 250 and 300 career victories. His excellence was further recognized in 1979 when he was honored as the National High School Athletic Coaches Association District 6 Coach of the Year, a distinction he accepted during ceremonies in Florida. Wollenweber’s achievements continued to draw statewide and national attention. He received the Metro League Hall of Fame award in 1984, followed by a national high school coaching gold award in 1987. The CHSCA Hall of Fame inducted him in 1989, and he was named Colorado’s Athletic Director of the Year for the 1988–89 school year. Before beginning his distinguished coaching career, Wollenweber competed as a member of the 1949 Northern Colorado baseball team that advanced to the NCAA Regionals, where the squad fell in a three‑game series to Southern California. Among the highlights of his coaching journey was the opportunity to coach his sons during his years at Sheridan in the 1960s and 1970s. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bert Borgmann
wordpress.contentText: Bert Borgmann Class of 2024 A proud graduate of Broomfield High School, Bert Borgmann dedicated his career to championing high school athletics and activities in Colorado. His journey began in the sports information offices at Colorado State University and the University of Northern Colorado, before embarking on a decades-long career with CHSAA. Over his tenure, Bert oversaw nearly every CHSAA-sanctioned sport as well as music, becoming a cornerstone of the organization. Revered for his institutional knowledge and passion for storytelling, Bert became the caretaker of CHSAA’s rich history and a trusted leader within the Association and at the National Federation of State High School Associations (NFHS). Former CHSAA commissioner Bob Ottewill praised Bert’s character, saying, “He never hesitated to take on any assignment – from basketball czar to snow shoveling duties – and did so with enthusiasm, a desire to learn and improve, and an uncommon ability to work with athletes, coaches, officials, event managers, and even parents.” Nationally, Bert served with distinction on numerous committees, including chairing the NFHS National Records Committee and contributing to the NFHS Baseball and Softball Rules Committees, Sportsmanship Committee, and National High School Hall of Fame Screening Committee. He also helped institutionalize the annual sports information directors gathering at the NFHS summer meeting — a tradition that fostered community and camaraderie across the country. Bert's commitment and excellence earned him numerous accolades, including: - The Jim Saccomano Award (National Football Federation – Colorado Chapter) - The Ray Lutz Award (Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference) - Induction into the Colorado Dugout Club Hall of Fame - The NFHS Section 6 “Citation Award” - Lifetime Honorary Membership in the IAABO Board 4 “His heart was so big. Bert was everyone’s biggest cheerleader and he helped make CHSAA what it is today,” said a colleague. “He was an incredible advocate for high school athletics, activities, and media. His legacy will live on for many years to come.” Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bill Helman
wordpress.contentText: Bill Helman Class of 1991 William “Bill” Helman made a lasting impact on Metropolitan State College athletics, where his leadership as a baseball coach and athletic director helped elevate the program to new levels. His influence was affirmed in April 1995 when the athletic department retired his baseball jersey, No. 1—only the second jersey ever retired in school history. Bill joined Metro State in 1979 as head baseball coach and remained in that role through 1992 before devoting his full attention to serving as athletic director. He compiled a career coaching record of 338–293–2 and consistently fielded competitive, high‑scoring teams. Under his guidance, the Roadrunners captured the NAIA team batting title in 1981 and finished as runners‑up in 1987. More than 50 of his players earned All‑American, all‑region, or all‑league recognition, and several moved on to professional baseball. A native of disciplined leadership, Bill earned his bachelor’s degree in business administration from The Citadel in 1967 and then served nine years in the U.S. Air Force, resigning with the rank of Captain. He later completed two master’s degrees from the University of Northern Colorado—one in recreation and administration in 1977 and another in business administration in 1978. Bill’s contributions extended far beyond wins and losses. He founded the Metro State Indoor Youth Baseball Clinic, played a key role in establishing the Denver Cup–City Championships from 1987–1992, and was recognized by the CHSBUA for Outstanding Service to Colorado High School Baseball in 1990. Throughout his career, he remained a dedicated advocate and fundraiser for the Metro State baseball program, leaving a meaningful legacy for generations of student‑athletes. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bill Lind
wordpress.contentText: Bill Lind Class of 1999 Bill Lind grew up playing baseball in Windsor, Colorado, before serving four years in the United States Navy. He later attended UNC in Greeley, earning a B.A. in 1964 and an M.A. in 1967. His early career included teaching and coaching basketball, baseball, and football. Bill began his umpiring journey in 1972, working High School Baseball in La Junta. He spent about four years in Lamar, where he officiated Junior College round‑robin tournaments. He continued to umpire high school games throughout the La Junta area for roughly six years. In 1979, Bill moved to Colorado Springs, returning to umpiring the following year. He worked there from 1980–1996 and served as area director for the Colorado Springs group, contributing to several state clinics. In addition to baseball, he officiated both basketball and football for many years. Bill deeply valued his time in athletics and cherished the many meaningful experiences he gained across all the sports he served. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box 1999 Hall of Fame Inductee Bill Lind – Colorado Springs Bill Lind played baseball in Windsor Colorado and was in the Navy for four years. He attended UNC in Greeley Colorado and graduated in 1964 with a BA and got an MA in 1967. Bill taught and coached Basketball, Baseball and Football. Bill’s experience as an umpire started with High School Baseball in La Junta in 1972. He worked games in Lamar for about four years doing Junior College games during round robin tournaments. Bill did High School games in and around La Junta for about six years. He then moved to Colorado Springs in 1979 and started back umpiring there in 1980 and worked until 1996. Bill served as area director for the Colorado Springs group and helped with a few state clinics. He also officiated Basketball and Football for a number of years. Bill enjoyed the time spent with athletics and have had many wonderful experiences with all of the sports.

wordpress.name: Bill Vincent
wordpress.contentText: Bill Vincent Class of 1990 Bill Vincent was born on May 12, 1927, in Denver, Colorado, and spent his life dedicated to education and amateur sports. A graduate of Wheat Ridge High School in 1945, he went on to earn both bachelor’s and master’s degrees in education from the University of Denver, where he also played football. Bill taught in the Denver Public Schools for thirty‑four years before retiring in December 1984. Alongside his teaching career, he built a remarkable forty‑six‑year officiating career that spanned virtually all amateur levels. He served as the second president of the CHSBUA from 1975–1977 and co‑founded the Umpires Association of Colorado, later serving as assigning secretary for U.A.C. umpires and for the Northern League. Known by colleagues as “Wheels,” a nickname earned for his modest on‑field speed, Bill approached every assignment with dedication and enthusiasm. He also co‑founded the Carnation Bowl, a championship event supporting youth football in Jefferson County. Bill Vincent’s lasting impact on Colorado sports and education continues to be felt through the organizations he helped build and the many officials and students he inspired. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bob Ferguson
wordpress.contentText: Bob Ferguson Class of 1999 Bob Ferguson built a remarkable umpiring career that spanned every level of the sport, from T‑Ball through college baseball. Known for his reliability and dedication, he worked games in every kind of Colorado weather—snow, rain, wind, and sunshine—often joking that he was as dependable as the mailman. One of Bob’s most memorable stories came during a game at Rocky Mountain High School, when wind gusts were so strong that two sheets of 4×8 Cellotex blew across the field, forcing the game to be called. Another unforgettable moment occurred in the early 1970s during a matchup between Poudre and Boulder, when Bob moved to cover a play at third base and unexpectedly went face‑first into the dirt, a story he shared with characteristic humor. Throughout his career, Bob worked numerous State Playoff games and served for several years as Northern Area Director. He also collaborated closely with Fort Collins Parks and Recreation, contributing to the development of local athletic programs. Bob retired from both teaching and umpiring in 1989. Bob played an important role in developing new officials and proudly introduced Mike Brady to the profession. In retirement, he stayed active by driving a city bus three days a week and working for a drywall company, continuing the strong work ethic that defined his career. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bob Jones
wordpress.contentText: Bob Jones Class of 2013 - Kansas City, Missouri Bob Jones retired from the Kansas City, Missouri Police Department in 1995 and currently works at Raytown Middle School. He and his wife, Debbie, have been married for 38 years and have two children, Bryan and Kristen. Bob began his officiating career in 1978, working high school baseball games as well as assignments in Major League Baseball. From 1979 through 1991, he worked every Big 8 and Big 12 Tournament game played—121 consecutive postseason contests. In 1991, he became the first Supervisor for the Big 8 Conference and later continued in that role as it transitioned to the Big 12, serving until 1996. In 2001, Bob was named the Coordinator of Umpires for the Missouri Valley Conference. His distinguished career includes 15 NCAA Division I Regionals and Super Regionals, along with three NCAA Division I College World Series appearances. Bob has also been a longtime instructor and evaluator, working the Mile High Camp since 1980. His first involvement in Denver came as an NCAA Regional Advisor and Evaluator, and he played a pivotal role in helping three CHSBUA umpires earn NCAA Division I postseason assignments. More importantly, Bob helped inspire and shape what eventually became the Mile‑High Advanced Umpire Clinic and the UTEP Program. With his guidance, three individuals developed what has become one of the most respected umpire development systems in the country—now more than 25 years strong. Without Bob’s expertise, mentorship, and continued participation, neither program would have reached the level of success it enjoys today. Bob has helped train roughly 75 percent of the CHSBUA umpires currently registered in Denver. His mastery of all phases of umpiring—from mechanics to game and situation management—has helped more than 300 umpires improve their skills and elevate the overall quality of the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association. He remains a trusted evaluator, an exceptional instructor, and a friend to umpires throughout CHSBUA. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bob Meisner
wordpress.contentText: Bob Meisner Class of 2005 Robert “Bob” W. Meisner graduated from Berkley High School in Berkley, California, in 1953, where he excelled in baseball, football, and basketball. He earned All–North California honors in both baseball and basketball before continuing his athletic and academic journey at several institutions. Bob earned an associate degree from Oakland City College, played baseball at the University of Southern California, and went on to spend 10 years with the New York Yankees organization. He later completed both his bachelor’s and master’s degrees at the University of Northern Colorado in Greeley and earned an administration degree from Colorado State University. Bob’s career in education and athletics spanned decades and impacted countless young athletes. He taught and coached at Arvada High School, where he served as head baseball coach and also coached tennis and football. He founded the school’s girls softball program and was named Coach of the Year in 1972, 1977, and 1979. Bob additionally participated in numerous high school baseball clinics, including the University of Denver baseball clinic. For 25 years, Bob conducted annual tryout camps for the Major League Scouting Bureau, offering opportunities for high school, American Legion, and college players across Colorado, Wyoming, Kansas, and Montana. Throughout his scouting career, he worked with the Yankees, Reds, Cardinals, and Rangers organizations. Bob’s contributions extended beyond the baseball diamond. While serving in the U.S. Army, he won the Second Army Championship at Fort Knox in 1959. He also coached at the Irv Brown Baseball Schools during the 1960s and 1970s and spent 20 years as a scoring official at Arvada High School and the Colorado state wrestling tournaments. In 1996, the CHSBUA honored him with the Mr. Baseball Award. He also coached CHSAA Baseball All-Star Teams and contributed to the Denver Post All-Star Game. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bob Ottewill
wordpress.contentText: Bob Ottewill Class of 2006 Robert “Bob” Ottewill graduated from Huntington High School on Long Island, New York, in 1964. He earned a B.A. in English from Ohio Wesleyan University in 1968, then completed forty‑five hours toward a graduate degree in English Literature at the University of Denver from 1968–72. In 1987, he earned a Type D Certificate in M.Ed./Administration from Colorado State University. From 1968–72, Bob taught English and coached basketball at Kearney Junior High School in Commerce City. During 1970–72, he also worked as a part‑time sportswriter for The Denver Post. In 1972, he joined CHSAA as an Administrative Assistant, was promoted to Associate Commissioner in 1986, and became Aurora Commissioner in 1990. Bob’s leadership included service on National Federation of State High School Associations rules committees for basketball, football, baseball, and softball over a twenty‑year span. He also officiated high school and college basketball. Since 1970, he has served as Commissioner of the Colorado Whiffleball Federation, and in 1998 he was named an Honorary Chairman of the Colorado All‑State Games. Throughout his career, Bob earned significant recognition, including the Al Schalge Memorial Award in 1995, a Certificate of Appreciation from the Denver Association of Black Basketball Officials in 1994, and the Friend of Broadcasting Award from the BA in 1990—the same year he was inducted into the Metro League Hall of Fame. Under his CHSAA tenure, he helped expand opportunities for student‑athletes by adding numerous sports, including girls swimming, skiing, basketball, cross country, softball, soccer, golf, field hockey, girls and boys lacrosse, volleyball, and ice hockey. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bruce Bradshaw
wordpress.contentText: Bruce Bradshaw Class of 2011 Bruce Bradshaw was born on March 15, 1957, in Pueblo, Colorado, to Janice and Robert Bradshaw, Sr. He graduated from Central High School and later earned a master’s degree in education and mass communications. Bruce lived his entire life in Pueblo until his unexpected passing on June 8, 2010. Bruce was a lifelong sports enthusiast. During high school, he played basketball and football and spent his summers on the baseball field. In later years, when time allowed, he enjoyed golf, traveling, and photography. A respected educator, Bruce taught English for 20 years at Wasson High School in District 11, where he was also active in the AVID program. He coached boys’ basketball and served as the school’s golf coach, making a lasting impact on countless students and athletes. Bruce began umpiring for CHSBUA in 1992 and went on to serve as the Area 3 Director for approximately six years. He also officiated several Colorado State Championships, earning the respect of peers, coaches, and players across the region. For more than 15 years, Bruce dedicated countless hours to Runyon Field, serving as the assignor for the Andenucio Baseball Tournaments. His involvement made him a central figure in Southern Colorado baseball, and he became a well‑known and trusted presence at tournaments throughout the state. Bruce Bradshaw’s integrity, dedication, and love for the game left a lasting legacy in both the classroom and on the baseball field. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bruce Grigsby
wordpress.contentText: Bruce Grigsby Class of 2012 Bruce Grigsby was born in Glendale, California, on September 7, 1946. He graduated from Burbank High School in 1964 and went on to earn his bachelor’s degree from California State University, Northridge in 1969. In 1995, he completed his master’s degree at St. John’s College in Santa Fe, New Mexico. Bruce enjoyed a long and distinguished career as both an umpire and an educator. As a teacher at Montrose High School, he was known for his passion for teaching and his deep compassion for his students. Many of them have said he was the best teacher they ever had. Bruce loved the opera and frequently organized trips for students to attend performances at the Metropolitan Opera or Broadway shows in New York City—experiences that opened their eyes to new cultures, music, and travel opportunities. Bruce was always thoroughly prepared, especially when it came to officiating. He studied the rules extensively, understanding them inside and out, and had a remarkable ability to translate the language of the rulebook into clear, visual explanations that anyone could understand. He was an outstanding mentor to new and inexperienced officials and consistently demonstrated the highest level of respect toward the athletes he served. Over the course of his career, Bruce officiated countless games in both baseball and basketball. He also contributed as a football coach and spent several years as a head baseball coach. As a husband, father, educator, friend, and exceptional baseball umpire, Bruce Grigsby exemplified character, dedication, and professionalism. His lifelong commitment to students and sports officials has earned him his well‑deserved place in the Umpires Baseball Hall of Fame. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bud Best
wordpress.contentText: Bud Best Class of 1996 Warren P. “Bud” Best built a distinguished career as an educator, coach, and official following his service in the South Pacific during World War II as a flight engineer on a Navy patrol bomber. After completing his military duty, he attended Wayne State College Nebraska, where he was a four‑year letterman in football, basketball, and track, and held the school’s 100‑yard dash record for more than thirty years. Bud went on to coach at both the high school and college levels, serving as a head coach in football, basketball, swimming, and track. A versatile athlete himself, he also played semi‑professional baseball. His transition into officiating began in 1961 after working as a coach on the Colorado State College baseball team. He went on to umpire numerous high school and American Legion postseason and State Tournament games. From 1967–1985, Bud served as an educational administrator at the University of Northern Colorado, all while earning recognition as an exceptional college and high school football and basketball official. His commitment to athletics and officiating has been honored by both the Wayne State Hall of Fame and the Greeley Baseball Umpires Association, acknowledging his many years of service and dedication as an assignor and official. Bud Best’s lifelong contributions reflect a career defined by leadership, integrity, and a deep devotion to student‑athletes and the officiating community. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Bud Schoepflin
wordpress.contentText: Bud Schoepflin Class of 2004 Earl “Bud” Schoepflin was born and raised in the Denver area and graduated from St. Joe’s High School in 1951. Shortly after, he enlisted in the Marine Corps, serving from 1951–1954 in a helicopter squadron. During his service, he dedicated time to the gym, developing a lifelong commitment to fitness. Bud often emphasized that officials owe athletes their very best, which begins with staying physically prepared. Known for his strength and presence on the field, Bud earned the nickname “Popeye” for his impressive “pipes.” While he expected professionalism and didn’t hesitate to enforce it, he was equally known for listening to explanations—before jokingly threatening to “whip you again.” His balanced approach and fairness made him widely respected across levels of play. Bud served as a state officer for both the baseball umpires association and the football officials association, and he worked as an assigner for the WAC and RMAC. His long affiliation with American Legion State Tournaments took him across Colorado, where he addressed dignitaries, umpired games, and evaluated fellow officials. He had a unique gift for communication, mentoring countless officials and teaching effective ways to work with coaches, players, and partners. Across his career, Bud was selected for approximately twenty‑two State High School Tournaments in all classifications, in addition to numerous American Legion and Connie Mack state and regional tournaments. At the college level, he umpired in the Big 8 Conference for 10 years, the RMAC for 12 years, and the WAC for 11 years, including assignments in California and Hawaii. He also worked the JUCO World Series in Grand Junction for 14 years. Bud married Bev on May 15, 1953, and together they raised six children and welcomed many grandchildren. To many, he was more than an official—he was a mentor, a friend, and a model of hard, honest work. Baseball is unquestionably better because of Bud Schoepflin. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Carl Zele Jr
wordpress.contentText: Carl Zele Jr Class of 2008 Carl L. Zele, Jr. graduated from Englewood High School in 1972 and began umpiring the following year while attending C.U. Boulder. Although he initially pursued a music career, his passion for athletics remained strong. After changing his major twice, he realized that baseball needed “more athletic umpires” and decided there was no better summer job than being paid to work on the field. One call to Bill Vincent set him on a new path, launching a distinguished umpiring career. From his first high school game in 1974 through 2002—when he was widely regarded as one of the finest overall athletes in the CHSBUA—Zele became a true student of the game. He sought insight from respected figures such as Irv Brown, Dal Ward, Coach Dave Wardell, and Frank “Chief” Prentup, while also learning through experiences alongside professional umpires Jon McSherry, Eric Gregg, Nick Bremigan, Joe Brinkman, and Rich Garcia. During this period, he also served ten years as a Colorado High School Basketball referee and officiated with the CHSGSUA through 2004. After attending the Bill Kinnamon Pro-Umpire School in 1978, Zele began working an extraordinary number of games. His experience spanned playing, coaching, and umpiring league finals, State Championships, district and regional tournaments, and numerous national events. He worked extensively with colleagues such as Ken Zenter, Willie Milton, Joe Bellich, Nick Emeterio, and Floyd Ewing. Throughout his career, he carried himself with poise and dignity, guided by the code of ethics he believed essential to baseball and the CHSBUA: never cancel a game unless sick or deceased, always strive to be the best—or at least look the best—and remember that maintaining calm is far harder, and far more important, than mouthing off. Through countless postseason assignments and a steadfast dedication to learning and improvement, Carl L. Zele, Jr. became one of the CHSBUA’s respected “Men in Blue,” helping uphold the standards that make the game strong. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Charlie Chainhalt
wordpress.contentText: Charlie Chainhalt Class of 1992 Charlie Chainhalt was a talented multi‑sport athlete who made his mark early at Denver Manual High School before continuing his baseball career at Colorado Teacher’s College (now the University of Northern Colorado). His skills caught the eye of legendary scout Cobe Jones, who signed him to a professional contract with the Giants organization. Chainhalt made his professional debut with Charlotte in the Carolina League and later spent time with the New York Giants, long enough—he liked to say—to “catch on to a few tricks.” He returned to Denver in 1946 and became a player‑coach for Joe Alpert’s team in the old Metro Denver Semi‑Professional League. He fondly recalled his days at Merchant’s Park in the Denver Post Tournament, including a memorable at‑bat against Satchel Paige: “I fouled one ball—that was it.” In 1977–78, Chainhalt coached the Axom Auto Company team to a National Championship, guiding future big leaguers such as Nick Capra and John Stearns. Known for his fiery personality, competitive spirit, and unfiltered style, he brought intensity and passion to every game. For Chainhalt, baseball was both profession and joy. His outlook captured his love for the game: “Baseball has always been a pleasure and also a job. I enjoy being around the kids.” Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Chris Dittman
wordpress.contentText: Chris Dittman Class of 2000 Chris Dittman began umpiring high school baseball in 1972. Over the next sixteen years, he emerged as one of the Denver Metro area’s most respected officials, working eight State Tournaments, including four 5A State Championship games. His peers recognized his leadership and skill when the Jefferson County Athletic League named him Baseball Official of the Year. A charter member of the Collegiate Baseball Umpires Association in 1979, Chris served the organization for nine years while helping train, recruit, and mentor many umpires who would later form the Colorado Collegiate Umpire Association. He often joked that he served as an “idol” to one umpire in particular, yet his steady guidance and veteran presence played a significant role in the CCUA’s formation. That group would become the source of officials for the Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference, Mountain West Conference, Western Athletic Conference, and Big XII Conference. After retiring from active umpiring, Chris transitioned into coaching and administration at Regis University, serving as Head Baseball Coach and later as Director of Athletics. In that role, he was an influential voice in creating the RMAC baseball conference, a development that opened doors for many Denver-based umpires to advance into collegiate baseball. Chris’s professional path brought him back to where it all began. A standout at Westminster High School, he went on to earn a bachelor’s degree from Wichita State University in 1967 and a master’s degree from The University of Colorado in 1971. He later taught and coached baseball at Ranum High School, then returned to Westminster as athletic director. He currently serves as principal of Westminster High School, completing a full-circle career rooted in service, leadership, and dedication to Colorado baseball. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Chuck Denney
wordpress.contentText: Chuck Denney Class of Pre-1990 Charles (Chuck) A. Denney Jr. lived a life marked by passion, service, and a deep commitment to those around him. Widely admired in his community, he dedicated his career to the broadcasting industry in both Lamar and La Junta, Colorado. As the owner of the La Junta station, he became a familiar voice and presence, contributing to sports broadcasting, hosting morning talk shows, and supporting countless local initiatives. Chuck’s professional achievements were significant. He was honored as Colorado’s Broadcaster of the Year, served as president of the Colorado Broadcasters Association, and contributed his expertise as a member of the selection committee for the Colorado Sports Hall of Fame. Beyond broadcasting, he devoted himself to public service, serving as mayor of La Junta and as an Otero County commissioner for eight years. A committed Rotarian since 1969, Chuck embodied the organization’s mission. He received Rotary International’s prestigious “Service Above Self” award—an honor given to only 150 members worldwide each year. His work included helping organize and fundraise for the delivery of 2,800 wheelchairs to individuals in need in Mexico and participating in efforts to administer polio vaccines to children in Egypt. Chuck proudly served in the 101st Airborne Division of the Army from 1953–1955. Raised in Columbus, Ohio, he was an avid Ohio State fan and a lifelong supporter of baseball and golf. He umpired for several years and was selected to officiate a Colorado State High School baseball championship game, a highlight of his time in athletics. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Cliff Hendrick
wordpress.contentText: Cliff Hendrick Class of 1997 H. Clifford Hendrick emerged in the 1970s as one of the pioneering “young lions” in Colorado officiating. A standout basketball player at Roaring Fork High School in Carbondale and the son of the town doctor, he joined the CHSBUA in 1970 and quickly earned recognition as the association’s top rookie umpire. Hendrick’s postseason career began in 1971, marking the start of an unbroken run of tournament assignments through his retirement from high school baseball in 1990. He worked the State Tournament from 1982–90, including five State Championship games. At the collegiate level, he officiated in the Big 8 and WAC, highlighted by his assignment to the 1986 Western Athletic Conference Championship Series in San Diego, California. Beginning in 1983, Hendrick also served nine years as an alternate umpire for the American Association (Professional AAA), working alongside future major‑league umpires such as Drew Coble. His administrative contributions were equally significant. He supervised umpires for The University of Wyoming, assigned the High School All‑Star Games, organized Honorary Group inductions, served as Denver area director for the CHSBUA, and supervised officials in both the Skyline League and Front Range League. Away from the field, Hendrick dedicated his professional life to education, serving as a science teacher in the Cherry Creek Schools beginning in 1974. His impact on Colorado officiating and youth development remains deeply respected. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Dan Cholas
wordpress.contentText: Dan Cholas Class of 2014 Dan grew up in the small mining and fruit‑growing town of Paonia. He graduated in 1961, earning All‑Conference and All‑State honors and capturing a State Championship in the discus. A four‑year, four‑sport athlete, he began his college career at Mesa Junior College in Grand Junction. After two strong seasons as the Mavericks’ fullback and kicker, he transferred to the University of Utah and later completed his collegiate career at what is now CSU–Pueblo , where he enjoyed two outstanding years of football. Dan graduated from CSU–Pueblo in 1966 and received multiple tryout offers as both a placekicker and running back. He chose the Denver Broncos in 1966 and again in 1968. When the dream of playing professional football ultimately came to an end, Dan transitioned to education—becoming a teacher, coach, and athletic director for 32 years, including 29 in the Delta School District. From 1970 to 1973, he served as the head football and baseball coach at Ignacio High School. After returning to Delta, he began coaching at the freshman level and eventually moved up to head football coach and assistant baseball coach. During his tenure as Athletic Director at Delta High School, Dan served as tournament director for the 1‑A Mountain League Baseball and Volleyball Tournaments for 25 years and directed several Regional Baseball Tournaments. Dan also built an impressive officiating career. He umpired for 40 years and spent 11 of those attending the Arizona Umpiring Academy. He was honored as a Stagg Member and received a lifetime scholarship. He worked three State Championship Finals along with numerous semifinal and regional games. In 1998 and 1999, Dan umpired in two Babe Ruth World Series events in Florida and served as UIC in 1999. Dan credits his greatest support to his family—his wife Doris, son Peter, and daughter Danna—whose encouragement and perspective shaped his teaching, coaching, and officiating career. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Dan Weikle
wordpress.contentText: Dan Weikle Class of 2024 To say that Dan Weikle’s umpiring career—beginning in 1975 and still ongoing—is filled with accomplishments would be an understatement. As CHSBUA President from 2000–2003, Dan focused on elevating umpiring standards through consistent training and evaluation. His philosophy was simple: provide every CHSBUA umpire with a clear path to advance their skills and meet, if not exceed, expected standards of performance. The foundation for CHSBUA’s advanced training and professional standards was modeled after the NCAA Umpire Development Program. Nearly 40 years ago, Dan and Keith Bailey attended the first NCAA National Umpire Development meeting in Kansas City, MO, braving the dead of winter. The following year, with support from Ray Belfiore, they successfully convinced NCAA Umpire Development to bring the training to Denver—an effort intended to spare Colorado umpires the icy hazards Dan and Bailey had endured on I-70 in January. Dan took this initiative further by inviting Jon Bible, the first NCAA Umpire Coordinator, to Denver to meet with interested umpires—some on the verge of entering the college ranks, and many seeking to sharpen their CHSBUA-level skills. After an open-invitation meet-and-greet dinner, Bible attended local Connie Mack and Stan Musial summer games to observe some of Colorado’s top CHSBUA umpires. Fast forward a few years, and Dan, Keith, Ray, and Jim Paronto established the Mile High Advanced Umpire Clinic (MHAUC). The clinic featured instruction from some of the most respected umpires in the nation, including Bob Jones, the first NCAA Umpire Development representative assigned to the Midwest, who served in a similar capacity to Bible. The clinic quickly became a success—eventually, more than two-thirds of CHSBUA umpires selected for high school postseason assignments received their continued professional development through MHAUC. Although Dan’s on-field résumé never matched that of many MHAUC graduates, his greatest contribution was clear: creating opportunities for umpires to learn, grow, and refine their craft. In the late 1980s, he approached Denver Zephyrs General Manager Tom Mahoney and requested permission for advanced-training umpires to attend a game or two to study professional umpires in action. Instead, Mahoney granted season passes to all umpires in the development program. Later, as a CHSBUA Area Director, Dan organized and hosted Denver-area umpire meetings in a suite at Mile High Stadium, coordinated with Zephyrs home games. Across a career spanning six decades, Dan created and led numerous instructional clinics, both locally and nationally. The Mile High Advanced Umpire Clinic was one of his earliest accomplishments. After establishing MHAUC and CHSBUA’s Umpire Training, Evaluation, and Promotion (UTEP) program, Dan went on to design a national training and evaluation system in his role as NCAA Division II National Umpire Coordinator (2010–2016). His mandate from the NCAA was to ensure top umpires nationwide were observed, evaluated, and promoted to the eight Division II postseason tournaments—including the national championship. To fulfill this mandate, Dan created two national NCAA clinics: The Palm Beach Challenge in Florida and The Desert Challenge in Phoenix. Both clinics featured the first-ever Division II Regional Advisors—Tim Hatfield, Dennis Whalen, and Kirk Knowles—all former MHAUC instructors and CHSBUA members. Their work became instrumental in the selection process for naming umpire crews for the NCAA Division II National Championship. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Dave "Chick" Baker
wordpress.contentText: Dave "Chick" Baker Class of 2023 Dave “Chick” Baker’s journey began in the small town of Snyder, Colorado, but his impact has stretched across continents and generations. In 1960, while stationed in Aviano, Italy as a member of the United States Air Force, he became a registered official with the Italian Sports Federation. Rather than soaring in the “Wild Blue Yonder,” Baker found his calling grounded on baseball diamonds, basketball courts, and volleyball courts—where his versatility and passion truly shined. Upon his final duty post at Lowry Air Force Base in Aurora, Colorado, Baker launched his Colorado officiating career in 1971—one that would span nearly fifty years and seven generations. Known for his consistency and professionalism, he officiated five state championship baseball games and numerous state football title contests. His reputation for excellence extended to basketball as well, where he contributed to 37 combined football and basketball postseason tournaments. A true pillar of CHSBUA, Baker served as President from 1984 to 1987, contributed on the Executive Committee, and dedicated decades as Secretary/Treasurer. He played a key role in organizing Master Clinics and was instrumental in supporting Joe Rossi in establishing the Hall of Fame. Dave “Chick” Baker exemplifies leadership, integrity, and dedication. His legacy is woven into the fabric of CHSBUA history, reminding us that from small-town beginnings come great and lasting contributions. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Dave Schmidt
wordpress.contentText: Dave Schmidt Class of 2011 David Schmidt was born on June 6, 1947, in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, to John and Marge Schmidt. He moved to Colorado in 1980 with Diane, and the two were married in 1982. Shortly afterward, they opened Stamp Store West, a packing and retail business. Their daughter, Mindy, was born in 1985. David passed away on May 31, 2008. David graduated from Chartiers Valley High School in 1965 and continued his education at Robert Morris College, earning a degree in education. He began his coaching and officiating career in Pennsylvania, where he coached Little League before expanding into umpiring both baseball and softball. After moving to Colorado, David became deeply involved in officiating and officiating leadership. He taught umpire school and served as an assignor for both the Metropolitan League and Skyline League. Over the years, he held multiple officiating roles, including baseball and softball umpire and basketball referee. David also managed and directed the annual 4th of July Tournament at Dakota Ridge High School, one of his signature contributions to the baseball community. Throughout his career, David earned several significant honors, including: Mr. Baseball Award, Colorado High School Baseball Coaches Association (2005) Honorary Member, Colorado High School Softball Umpire Association (2005) Distinguished Service Award (2006–2007) David Schmidt was widely respected for his leadership, his integrity, and his dedication to developing officials and supporting youth baseball. A fine man and a major contributor to the Colorado baseball community, his legacy continues to be felt today. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: David Letofsky
wordpress.contentText: David Letofsky Class of Pre-1990 Dave Letofsky was a pioneering figure in Aurora baseball, coaching alongside his twin brother Mert and helping establish a foundation for the city’s youth programs. Over his long career, he guided teams from the pee wee level through American Legion baseball, capturing numerous league, district, regional and state titles. A key leader in forming youth leagues across Aurora, Dave also organized opportunities for young athletes throughout the Denver area. He coached his three sons — Jeff, Larry and Brian — from the Jaycees level through American Legion competition, with all three later enjoying successful college athletic careers. Although baseball was his greatest passion, Dave also made significant contributions in basketball and football, coaching teams throughout Aurora. Known affectionately as “Big Dave,” he became an influential mentor to countless young players whose lives were shaped by his steady guidance and generous spirit. Dave also distinguished himself as an umpire, building and operating his own umpire organization. He officiated at every level, from state high school championships to NCAA Division I regionals, and was selected multiple times as an alternate for the College World Series staff. His abilities carried into softball as well, where he worked national fastpitch tournaments across the country. Dave passed away in 2008. He is survived by his wife, LaVerne, and six children — three daughters and three sons — leaving behind a lasting legacy of service, leadership and love for the game. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Dennis Smith
wordpress.contentText: Dennis Smith Class of 2017 Dennis Smith graduated from Rocky Ford High School , where he was a three‑sport athlete competing in baseball, football, and basketball. He continued his education at Otero Junior College before serving honorably in the United States Army , earning recognition as a Vietnam War veteran. Dennis is the longest‑serving County Treasurer in Colorado, holding the position for 38 years and counting. He and his wife, Cindy, are the proud parents of four children and grandparents to six. Dennis began his officiating career in 1975 and went on to contribute across multiple sports for several decades. In baseball, he worked for 32 years, including three CHSAA State Championship games. In basketball, he officiated for 38 years and worked 15 CHSAA State Tournaments. His football officiating career spanned 36 years and included three additional State Championship assignments. Dennis also officiated high school girls softball, spent more than 20 years involved in Babe Ruth Baseball, and worked two Babe Ruth World Series tournaments. A respected leader, Dennis served as an Area Director in all three major sports for more than 25 years. His dedication and impact earned him induction into both the Football and Basketball Halls of Fame, and he was honored with the Gene Bunelle Basketball Award in 2016. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Dick Cartin
wordpress.contentText: Dick Cartin Class of 1993 Richard “Dick” Daniel Cartin was born in Watertown, New York, on July 19, 1941, and proudly served four years in the US Navy. He spent most of his adult life in Lakewood, Colorado, where he dedicated three decades to teaching High School Alternative Education in Jefferson County. His impact as an educator was widely recognized, earning honors from News Center 4/Rocky Mountain News as a “Teacher Who Makes a Difference” and the Wheat Ridge Chamber of Commerce Outstanding Educator Award. During the 1970s, Dick became one of the founding members of the Jefferson County Colorado Sports Officials, helping establish a strong foundation for sports officiating in the region. After retiring, he moved to Cedaredge to be closer to the fishing he loved and quickly immersed himself in strengthening the community he grew to cherish. In Cedaredge, Dick served on the Cedaredge Board of Trustees and the Planning Commission, played an integral role in the Adopt-A-Highway program, and delivered meals to seniors and residents homebound by COVID. He continued his lifelong commitment to education by mentoring and developing alternative education programs for students. Dick found joy in the outdoors, especially cross‑country skiing, hiking, fishing, and camping on the Grand Mesa. His life reflected dedication to service, community, and the belief that one person can make a meaningful difference. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Dick Yates
wordpress.contentText: Dick Yates Class of Pre-1990 Dick Yates, a proud Denver native, built a coaching legacy that shaped generations of baseball and football athletes. He attended Denver South High School and the University of Denver before beginning a long and influential career as a teacher and coach within Denver Public Schools. Yates became the first head baseball coach at John F. Kennedy High School when it opened in 1966. Over a thirty‑five–year career — including twenty‑two years as a head coach in baseball and football — he established one of Colorado’s most consistent high‑school athletic programs. His teams earned 18 city championships, eight state championships, and two regional titles, including success in summer American Legion baseball. In recognition of his lasting impact on youth sports, Yates was inducted into the Colorado Sports Hall of Fame in 2007. His influence extended well beyond his own career. Sons Scott and Shannon, along with grandson Rich, have continued the family’s commitment to athletics. Scott, a former Kennedy state football champion and University of Colorado player, now serves as head football coach at Kent Denver, where his son Rich plays. Shannon starred on the 1986 Kent Denver team before competing at the United States Air Force Academy. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Don Carlsen
wordpress.contentText: Don Carlsen Class of 2006 Don H. Carlsen was born on October 15, 1926, in Chicago, Illinois, and passed away on September 23, 2002. He led a remarkable and varied life. After graduating from East High School in 1944, he served two and a half years in the Navy before beginning a six‑year professional baseball career. During this time, he attended Pepperdine College and later earned a B.S. from Denver University in 1956. Carlsen played in the major leagues with the Chicago Cubs and Pittsburgh Pirates, and in the minor leagues with Los Angeles and Hollywood in the AAA Coastal League, Tulsa in the Texas League, and New Orleans and Nashville in the Southern League. An elbow surgery ultimately ended his playing career. He also taught algebra at Smiley Junior High School in 1956 and worked alongside Bill Reed as a color commentator for the Denver Bears radio broadcasts in 1956–1957. His connection to umpiring began in high school when he worked summer games in the Old Timers League. His most memorable moment came when he filled in for an ill umpire during a Denver Bears game, leading to a lively dispute with opposing coach Warren Spahn—an incident fondly remembered for years. From 1954–1974, Carlsen coached Youth Baseball and Legion A for Burt Chevrolet, and he spent one year coaching semi‑pro baseball. He also served as a Big Brother for five years and was a member of its board. Carlsen’s contributions extended beyond the field. Alongside MLB relief pitcher Ryne Duren, he co‑founded the Ryne Duren Celebrity Golf Tournament, raising thousands of dollars for charitable causes from 1989–2000. His athletic accomplishments earned him numerous honors, including DAC Athlete of the Year in 1973, induction into the Colorado Handball Hall of Fame in 1999, and enshrinement in the Colorado Sports Hall of Fame in 2001. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Don Stengel
wordpress.contentText: Don Stengel Class of 2014 Don Stengel was a stand‑out high school baseball player at Highland High School (now Skyview), graduating in 1978. He now resides in Loveland with his wife, Patti, and is the proud father of five children: Candace, LeeAnn, Jennifer, Deidra, and Casey. Don played Independent Professional Baseball in Idaho Falls and later worked as a professional scout with both the Atlanta Braves and the Kansas City Royals. He also served as a head coach at South High School, Golden High School, and the Academy of Charter Schools. In addition, Don spent 12 years coaching with the Englewood Redbirds of the Stan Musial Semi‑Professional League, where he worked alongside the legendary Hans Robinson. Don began umpiring in 1985 with the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association and eventually served as an Area Director in 1989 alongside Dan Weikle and Don Rolfe. He was also a founding member of the Colorado Collegiate Umpire Association and worked collegiate baseball in the Western Athletic Conference and the Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference. A fixture in high school postseason tournaments, Don officiated six state championship games. One of his most memorable assignments was a 15‑inning battle between Lamar and La Junta, where he worked alongside crew mates Dan Ringsby and Dave Baker. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Doug Graham
wordpress.contentText: Doug Graham Class of 2016 Doug is a Colorado native and a graduate of Arvada West High School. He has built a distinguished career as a leader within CHSBUA , serving a total of 12 years in association leadership. He was elected Denver Area Director in 2000 and later served as CHSBUA President from 2008 to 2010. Doug also played a key role in developing the organization’s Umpire Rules Training Program, which continues to guide new officials today. As an umpire, Doug worked numerous postseason assignments, including three CHSAA State Championship Games. His officiating career also extended into college baseball, where he worked in the Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference and the Mountain West Conference . In addition to his contributions to umpiring, Doug served his country in the United States Air Force and is a veteran of the Iraq War. His service and dedication, both on and off the field, are deeply appreciated. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ed O'Connor
wordpress.contentText: Ed O'Connor Class of 2013 - Arvada Ed O’Connor graduated from Holy Family High School in 1971 and later attended Metro State College, where he majored in Business. Ed and his wife, Susan, reside in Arvada, Colorado. He continues to work for The Denver Post , where he has been a dedicated and valued employee since 1976. Ed began his officiating career in 1980 on the baseball diamond and continued through 2010. Over those three decades, he accumulated approximately 59 playoff assignments and worked three State Championships. He officiated at all levels of high school and Legion baseball, as well as several adult and semi‑professional leagues, including MSBL, NABA, Connie Mack, and Stan Musial. In addition to baseball, Ed had an extensive officiating career in other sports. He worked basketball from 1983 to 2008 and football from 1980 to 2012. His assignments included 2A and 3A State Championships, American Legion State Championship playoff games, and the Men’s Senior League from 1985 to 2010. In 2010, Ed outlined his personal goals for baseball officiating—goals that reflected his professionalism, humility, and commitment to growth: Study, learn, and fully understand the rules Establish and maintain proper game control Mentor and teach new officials Work effectively with diverse groups of people “Do not become an old‑fart official” Ed’s professional approach, strong work ethic, and dedication to mentoring others left a lasting impact on the many officials who had the privilege of working alongside him. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ed Underwood
wordpress.contentText: Ed Underwood Class of 1997 Edward “Eddie” Underwood was a respected Commander of Harold Dehaan Post #19 in Fort Morgan and a deeply committed supporter of American Legion Baseball. Known throughout the community for his tireless dedication, he devoted more than thirty‑five years as the local baseball chairman and fifteen years as the State Legion Chairman. In 1976, Underwood organized the State “C” program, opening the door for younger athletes to begin participating in Legion Baseball. Over his tenure, he helped organize twenty‑six State Legion Tournaments and became a driving force behind the success of the Fort Morgan program. He worked throughout the community to secure coaches, umpires, and sponsors, ensuring that every season was fully supported. To help fund the program, Underwood organized Bingo nights at the Legion Club on Sundays and Wednesdays and regularly sought donations from local businesses. He often said, “We do it for the kids,” always crediting the community and the Legion Post for keeping the program strong. Underwood’s connection to the program was personal as well—his three sons and two grandsons all played Legion Baseball in Fort Morgan. The American Legion Baseball community lost an influential and dedicated leader when he passed away on February 2, 1994. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Edward Stevens
wordpress.contentText: Edward Stevens Class of 1990 Ed Stevens began building his legacy early in life on the South Side of Chicago. After the death of his father, he left school at age thirteen to work full time and support his mother and two brothers. When he was nineteen, the family relocated to Golden, where Ed began a lifelong commitment to softball and baseball. He competed in one of Denver’s most competitive softball leagues before transitioning to umpiring and coaching. For more than three decades, he was regarded as one of Colorado’s top high school and college umpires. He also earned national recognition as an ASA fast pitch softball umpire, becoming the only man to both play in a national tournament (1957) and later umpire in one (1971). Ed served for many years on the board of directors for AABC baseball and acted as commissioner for numerous local leagues. He also directed countless state and regional tournaments, contributing significantly to the growth of organized baseball in the area. As one of the pioneers of Arvada Junior Baseball, he helped build opportunities for young athletes across the community. He coached his son’s 10‑year‑old team and later led the B Legion team at Arvada High School. During the summers, he guided his American Legion squads to back‑to‑back state championships in 1980 and 1981 — the first time in Colorado history a program achieved consecutive titles. Ed Stevens’ impact on youth baseball and officiating continues to shape the Arvada and Denver‑area sports community. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ernie Kozacek
wordpress.contentText: Ernie Kozacek Class of 2006 Ernest “Ernie” B. Kozacek graduated from Merrit Hutton High School in Thornton in 1967 before earning his B.S. from Western State College in 1971. He entered the officiating ranks in 1972, just as CHSAA began organizing officials across all sports. Known affectionately as “Big E,” Ernie officiated high school baseball from 1972–2003. He worked under early assignors such as Bill Vencent and Mel Schwartz in Jeffco, Mr. Wilson in Denver, and Julie Carrabello at Metro. During those years, officials earned seven dollars and fifty cents for varsity games and four dollars for junior varsity, and the uniform consisted of a white shirt and tie with a double‑breasted jacket and slacks. Over the next three decades, Ernie built a respected schedule that included numerous district assignments and several regional games. He also served as Assignor for the United Pacific League and Mile Hi League for many years, and he became the dependable replacement official whenever needed. Ernie remained grateful throughout his career, valuing the many relationships and experiences that shaped his long tenure in Colorado high school baseball. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ernie Ortiz
wordpress.contentText: Ernie Ortiz Class of 2008 Ernie Ortiz brought a lifetime of discipline and leadership from his military service into an exceptional career as a sports official. A retired Army Sergeant First Class, he first distinguished himself in the “Squared Circle” as an award‑winning boxer. Early in his athletic career, he even faced fellow standout Richard “The Hawk” Hawkins—an early meeting between two competitors who would later form a respected umpiring duo in the Denver Metro area. Ernie’s preparation for officiating deepened during the Korean Conflict, where he served as a military policeman stationed in Hawaii. His athletic success in the service opened the door to sports officiating, and during his second tour in Europe he was assigned as a military official. He excelled in this role, earning selection to an elite crew that worked games featuring major league ballplayers completing their military obligations. He also officiated contests involving the Army, Navy, Marines, the University of Hawaii, and teams from the Caribbean. During his final tour in 1966, Ernie served in Cuchi, Viet Nam, training young recruits in guerrilla warfare tactics. Upon entering civilian life, he quickly became a respected arbiter in the Denver area, working high school and college baseball and softball. He was a dedicated member of the Colorado High School Baseball Umpire Association and a charter member of the Colorado Collegiate Umpire Association. Today, Ernie remains active and says “the wheels are still working,” though he misses the camaraderie and competition on the field. His lifetime of service and officiating continues to serve him well as he and his wife, Eva, enjoy a large and lively family that includes eight children, twenty‑two grandchildren, and six great‑grandchildren. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box
