wordpress.name: Frank DeAngelis
wordpress.contentText: Frank DeAngelis Class of 2009 Frank DeAngelis graduated from Ranum High School in Westminster, Colorado, in 1971. He went on to earn a B.A. from Metropolitan State College in 1978, an M.A. from the University of Colorado Denver in 1984, and a professional certificate from the University of Phoenix in 1994. Frank and his wife, Evelyn, currently reside in Thornton. Frank amassed 17 years of high school coaching experience in the Jefferson County School District. He served as a varsity assistant for one year at Jefferson Senior High, two years at Wheat Ridge Senior High, and another year at Columbine Senior High School. Frank later became the Head Baseball Coach at Columbine, where he led the program to three League Championships and two State Championships over his 13‑year tenure. His leadership extended well beyond the high school season. Frank coached Summer League Baseball for 17 years, guiding his teams to 10 League Championships and one State Championship. His accomplishments earned him numerous honors, including: National Coach of the Year Region 7 Baseball Coach of the Year, Colorado High School Coaches Association Rocky Mountain News & Denver Post Baseball Coach of the Year (two years) Jefferson County Board of Control Male Coach of the Year (three years) Jefferson County Baseball Coach of the Year Selected Coach, Colorado Rockies High School All‑State Game Six‑time Coach, Jefferson County All‑Star Team in the State All‑Star Game Mr. Baseball, Colorado High School Baseball Coaches Association University of Notre Dame – People Who Make a Difference recognition Colorado Athletic Directors Association Outstanding School Administrator of the Year Colorado Interscholastic Athletic Administrator Award Selected to carry the Olympic Torch during the 2002 Winter Olympic Torch Relay Jefferson County Association of Educational Office Professionals Administrator of the Year Frank also served in numerous leadership capacities that shaped baseball across Colorado. His extensive committee and organizational roles include: President, Colorado High School Baseball Coaches Association Director, Columbine and Chatfield Little League Baseball Camps Director, All‑State Baseball Games Colorado State Baseball Committee member Chairperson, CHSAA Baseball Committee Member, Classification and League Organizing Committee (CLOC) Coordinator, All‑State Baseball Games in collaboration with the Colorado Rockies Member, School Improvement Committee Regional Representative, American Baseball Congress Building Representative and Scholarship Committee Member, Colorado High School Coaches Association Frank DeAngelis’s contributions to baseball, education, and leadership at both the district and statewide levels are extraordinary. His influence continues to shape Colorado athletics, and his dedication to student‑athletes has left an enduring legacy throughout the state. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Frank Messenger
wordpress.contentText: Frank Messenger Class of 2000 Frank Messenger built a distinguished twenty‑seven‑year career as an umpire, working fields from Tobacco Road to South Boulder Road. Known for his steady leadership and deep knowledge of the game, he contributed extensively to the profession through committee work, training, and organizational service. Messenger’s umpiring résumé includes seven consecutive years of high school postseason assignments, earned while officiating in both North Carolina and Colorado. He served as President of the Rocky Mountain Umpires Association, sat on the board of directors of the Rocky Mountain Sports Officials Association, and was a member of the Colorado Baseball Umpires Association Executive Committee. Beyond baseball, he also officiated softball, working nine State Tournaments and four Regional Tournaments as part of the Amateur Softball Association, while the Denver Recreation Department relied on him to conduct umpire training clinics. After retiring from active umpiring, Messenger continued shaping youth athletics through a successful coaching career. He led girls basketball teams to two State Tournaments, earning Metropolitan League Coach of the Year honors in 1990 and 1991, and the Denver Post Coach of the Year award in 1990. He later added Metropolitan League Coach of the Year recognition in baseball in 1995. His coaching achievements were capped by his induction into the Metropolitan League Hall of Fame. Messenger’s work with young people extended beyond the court and diamond. He served as a camp counselor and recreation director, further demonstrating his commitment to youth development. A graduate of Regis High School, he earned his degree from Regis College (BA Sociology/PE) and holds a Colorado Teaching Certificate with endorsements in elementary education and junior high mathematics. Frank continues to teach and coach at Elbert High School, and he and his wife, Nancy, make their home in Elbert, Colorado. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Frank Prentup
wordpress.contentText: Frank Prentup Class of Pre-1990 Frank “Chief” Prentup, a full-blooded Tuscarora Iroquois, was born in 1907 in Leavenworth, Kansas, to Lafayette F. Printup and Augusta M. Printup. A clerical error on his birth certificate resulted in the misspelling of his last name. He graduated from Junction City High School in 1926 and went on to earn both bachelor’s and master’s degrees in physical education from the Kansas State College of Agriculture and Applied Science. During his collegiate years, he lettered in football as a quarterback, in baseball as a second baseman, and in swimming. From 1932–40, Prentup coached multiple sports in the Kansas High School system. He earned such lasting respect at Manhattan High School that students, faculty, and staff successfully championed a change of the school’s mascot from the Manhattan Blues to the Manhattan Indians. He joined the University of Colorado in 1941 as an assistant coach in football, baseball, and basketball. In 1943, he entered the United States Navy, serving as a training officer at the Naval Flight School in the Norfolk, Virginia, area until his discharge in 1945. Prentup returned to Boulder that same year, continuing as an assistant coach in football and baseball while serving as an associate professor in physical education. He was named head baseball coach at CU in 1947, a position he held for twenty-two seasons. He compiled a 257–255–2 record, guiding the 1946 team to a Big Seven Conference co‑championship and leading the 1961 team to a third‑place finish in the Big Eight Conference. Among his many honors, Prentup received the Meritorious Service Award from the American Baseball Coaches Association in 1986. Prentup Field, built in 1967 on CU’s east campus, was named in his honor and was regarded as the premier baseball facility in the Big Eight Conference. He was inducted into the University of Colorado Athletic Hall of Fame in 2015. A lifelong educator and innovator, Prentup co‑authored Scientific Techniques of Batting in 1973 and wrote Punching the Light Bag in 1985. He was a pioneer in the development of rope‑skipping training for agility and stamina, creating an accredited physical education course at CU and writing Skipping the Rope for Fun and Fitness in 1963. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Frank Zele
wordpress.contentText: Frank Zele Class of 1990 Frank William Zele Jr. was born in Hastings, Colorado, and graduated with the Trinidad High School class of 1937. He went on to earn degrees from Trinidad State Junior College and the University of Denver, where he completed a B.A. in Accounting. Frank served in the United States Navy from 1937–1945. Following his military service, he worked at the Air Force Finance Center in Denver and later with the Colorado State Department of Labor in Lakewood until his retirement. A dedicated official, Frank umpired for 38 years and remained an active rancher throughout his life. His long-standing commitment to his community and to the game left a lasting impact on those who worked alongside him. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Fred Zuercher
wordpress.contentText: Fred Zuercher Class of 2026 Fred Zuercher began his umpiring career in the late 1970s in the Bay Area of California, working little league games in and around San Jose. His story mirrors that of many umpires. “I started on a kind of a dare,” he recalls. “Somebody said, ‘Why don’t you try this?’” And, as Fred puts it, “It all fell into place.” Fred quickly advanced—far beyond “doing pretty well.” His rise took him from little league to high school baseball, then to junior college, and eventually into the PAC‑8 Conference, a premier Division I baseball conference featuring programs such as the University of Southern California and Arizona State University. He credits much of this early success to being in the right place at the right time and to knowing the right people, including Gus Rodriguez, who later became an umpire supervisor for the International Baseball Federation, and Dwight Furgeson, an NFL referee who coordinated officials for multiple sports through the FERMAR Sports Association. He also learned quickly through what he calls a “painful finishing school.” “Working college ball in California, you knew a coach would come out and have a little session with you. You knew it was coming,” Fred said. His time in California included working a California High School State Baseball Championship and seeing future stars like Barry Bonds in their early years. “I thought he was never going to make it. He was a scrawny little (devil) in high school,” Fred joked. Away from the field, Fred built an impressive military career. He retired as a Senior Master Sergeant after serving with the Nevada Air National Guard . When he later moved to Colorado, his military bearing—combined with his umpiring résumé—quickly drew attention. “Military regimentation taught me a lot about how to do it,” he explains. His skills were soon recognized, and he was assigned to work three additional CHSAA State Baseball Championships in Colorado. A key influence during this period was Ross Barlow, a previous Rossi/ CHSBUA inductee, who helped Fred understand the competitive landscape of Colorado umpiring. “I thought I’d get into the Air Force Academy (Division I baseball), but there were twenty or thirty guys ahead of me. Barlow got me into junior college.” Although he entered the collegiate ranks, Fred never left high school baseball. Over the years, he became a highly respected member of CHSBUA . In addition to serving as the Area 2 umpire assigner, he was repeatedly elected to what was originally intended as a two‑year term as the Colorado Springs Area Director—a role he ended up holding for more than twenty‑seven years. In this time, Fred trained and mentored hundreds of new officials. No longer calling balls and strikes, Fred still dedicates himself to the game by attending contests and evaluating developing umpires. What does he look for in successful officials? “First and foremost, I attend their pregame to figure out their attitude. Attitude is big for me—confident, but not overconfident. Too many guys talk a good game but don’t apply themselves.” He looks for individuals who share the mindset he once held: “I am going to be the best high school official I can be.” Fred also takes pride in his work as an assigner. “I’d like to think I’m very good at the assigning, which is becoming more and more difficult.” As his nomination letter states, “Fred continues to be a beacon of information for all and is always willing to help where needed.” Reflecting on his long and successful career, Fred simply notes, “It all fell into place.” Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Fritz Johnstone
wordpress.contentText: Fritz Johnstone Class of 2010 Frederick “Fritz” R. Johnstone was born on December 14, 1944, in Seattle, Washington. He graduated from Sauk Rapids High School in Sauk Rapids, Minnesota, in 1963. In his later years, Fritz lived in Sterling, Colorado, with his wife, Sharon, until his passing on November 15, 2009. Fritz officiated high school sports for more than 30 years, including basketball and football, before becoming a baseball umpire in 1979—an avocation he continued throughout his entire life. During his officiating career, he served as an Area Director in all three sports and became a respected teacher, coach, and mentor to countless new officials across Colorado. Fritz worked numerous District, Regional, and State Tournaments and was a dedicated presence in summer baseball. He officiated Legion and other local summer programs and also served as an assignor for many Northeast Colorado high schools and seasonal leagues. Even after retiring from active officiating, Fritz never stepped away from the game. He regularly attended NJC baseball games and even drove the team bus, including on their annual spring trip to Arizona. Until the day he passed, Fritz rarely missed a local high school game—always eager to watch, evaluate, and support the officials working on the field. Fritz Johnstone’s lifelong dedication to Colorado athletics, his unwavering presence in the baseball community, and his commitment to mentoring new officials have firmly earned him a place in the Umpires Hall of Fame. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Gary Baker
wordpress.contentText: Gary Baker Class of 2003 Gary Baker graduated from Cathedral High School, where he excelled in football, basketball, and baseball. He continued his education at the University of Denver, playing freshman football in 1960—the final year the university fielded a football program. In 1961–62, Gary attended Mesa Junior College in Grand Junction and played football during the 1961 season. He returned to the University of Denver, earning a Bachelor of Arts in Education in 1964. Gary began his coaching career at Saint Francis High School in Denver from 1965–66. He then coached at Holyoke High School in 1966–67 and later returned to Cathedral High School during the same period. His career with Aurora Public Schools spanned 1969–80, with assignments at Aurora Central, Aurora Hinkley, and the district’s junior high programs. In addition to coaching, Gary contributed significantly as an official. He umpired high school baseball for 15 years and refereed boys and girls high school basketball for 18 years. He also officiated at the NCAA level for six years. His umpiring experience included one summer in the Grand Junction Semi-Pro League and a season in the Alaska Semi-Pro League, where he worked alongside Emmett Ashford. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Gene Bunnelle
wordpress.contentText: Gene Bunnelle Class of 1990 Francis “Gene” Bunnelle graduated from Rankin Township High School in 1945 before earning his bachelor’s degree from Buena Vista College in 1950 and his master’s degree from Western State College in 1959. He went on to make a lasting impact in Colorado education and activities administration, becoming a respected and influential figure in the Colorado High School Activities Association. Gene began his career on the Western Slope, serving as a teacher and coach at Fruita Junior High School and later as principal at Roaring Fork High School in Carbondale. In 1965 he joined the CHSAA, where he spent twenty‑four years providing administrative leadership across nearly every sport and activity sanctioned by the Association. His commitment to Colorado students was especially evident in his leadership of the CHSAA’s student‑council workshops and conferences, which became essential programs for developing student leadership statewide. Gene was also active in officiating and rules interpretation in multiple sports, earning broad respect for his knowledge and steady guidance. Throughout his distinguished career, Gene received numerous honors, including induction into several halls of fame and the National Federation of State High School Associations Distinguished Service Award in 1995. His legacy remains one of service, integrity, and unwavering dedication to students across Colorado. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Gene_Rozelle
wordpress.contentText: Hall of Fame Inductee Gene Rozelle — Grand Junction, Colorado Gene Rozelle was born in Danbury, Nebraska, and moved to Grand Junction as a child, where he attended local schools and graduated from Central High School in 1947. Shortly after graduation, Gene proudly served his country in the U.S. Air Force, spending four years stationed in France before returning to the Grand Valley. Gene’s passion for sports shaped his life and career. He became the beloved “Voice of JUCO,” serving as the radio play-by-play announcer for nearly four decades. His dedication and distinctive voice brought countless games to life, including every tournament game during his tenure. In recognition of his tremendous contributions, a plaque honoring Gene hangs in the press level of the Tower at Suplizio Field, and the baseball broadcast booth there proudly bears his name — the “Gene Rozelle Broadcast Booth.” Beyond his broadcasting legacy, Gene was known for his love of sports, his enjoyment of bingo, and his quiet moments spent fishing and painting. His lifelong dedication to the community and the game of baseball left an enduring mark on the Colorado baseball and umpiring world.

wordpress.name: George Demetriou
wordpress.contentText: George Demetriou Class of 2026 - Colorado Springs “I was ordered to become an official,” is how George Demetriou describes the beginning of his officiating journey. It started at West Point , where a strict cadet physical requirement forced underclassmen to participate in an intramural sport. By his senior year, George had a choice: run cross‑country through the forests surrounding the United States Military Academy in full combat gear, or become an intramural sports official. He chose the latter—trading olive drab for black‑and‑white stripes. After graduating from West Point, George—now a second lieutenant—received active‑duty orders. “I was in field artillery for twelve years,” he recalls. His career eventually brought him to Fort Carson in Colorado Springs, where he transitioned into a quieter assignment in financial management. Although he became the base comptroller, military life meant constant moves, and another relocation loomed. Faced with choosing between another transfer or providing stability for his family, George chose family. He retired from the Army, settled in Colorado Springs, found work in financial management, and—much to his wife’s surprise—became an umpire. George’s officiating career began simply: “I was coaching little league and wanted to umpire their practices.” From there, he discovered the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association (CHSBUA) and launched what would become a remarkable, even legendary, career. Driven by an analytical, almost scientific approach to officiating, George immersed himself in rules study. “I picked up one of Carl Childress’ books and said to myself, ‘I can do this.’” He also read Referee Magazine , eventually submitting the first of what would become more than a thousand articles. His early attempts at writing were met with heavy edits and discouraging feedback. “My editor redlined the shit out of it and sent it back,” George recalls. Although the article was eventually published, the editors warned him not to submit anything else unless requested. Soon after, however, the magazine’s rules writer resigned—and the editors came calling. George rejoined Referee Magazine “on active duty” in 1991 and has been a regular contributor ever since. His work helped shape the magazine’s evolving style, and his detailed investigative column, Doing It , has become one of its staples. His manuals, T he Study Guide for College Baseball Rules and Baseball Rules Differences, are now essential reading for high school and college baseball umpires nationwide. Within CHSBUA , George has been equally influential. He served as CHSBUA President (1997) and has been the Colorado State Rules Interpreter since 2001. He was a perennial selection for the CHSAA State Baseball Tournament from 1995 until his retirement in 2021. He is also a member of the Colorado High School Activities Association Hall of Fame and one of only three Colorado officials inducted into the National Federation of State High School Associations (NFHS) Hall of Fame , the governing body for high school sports nationwide. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Gordon Cooper
wordpress.contentText: Gordon Cooper Class of 2002 Gordon R. Cooper was born on April 1, 1930, in McGill, Nevada. He married Vonda G. (Larson) Cooper in 1951, and together they raised two sons, Gordon Steven (born 1957) and Shawn Roger (born 1959). Gordon graduated from White Pine County High School in Ely, Nevada, in 1948 and later earned his B.A. from the University of Denver in 1952. During his collegiate years, Gordon excelled in both football and baseball. He earned All-Conference honors in each sport for three seasons and was named second‑string All‑American in football as a senior. He also set an NCAA pass‑reception record with 106 catches over the three years ending in 1952. He was drafted in the seventh round in 1952 by the Detroit Lions, appearing in three exhibition games before entering the U.S. Army. After serving 18 months in Germany, he returned to the University of Denver to complete his master’s degree in 1956. Gordon began his teaching and coaching career at Rifle High School in the fall of 1956. Over three decades, he led programs in football, baseball, track, and wrestling. His teams earned a Track State Championship in 1963, a Football State Championship in 1973, and a tie for the Football State Championship in 1961. His baseball teams captured State Championships in 1981 and 1985. He also coached wrestling for several seasons, guiding four individual State Champions. Throughout his career, Gordon received significant recognition for his leadership and success. He was named AA Coach of the Year in baseball in 1981, 1984, and 1986. In 1987, he retired from Rifle High School after an influential tenure, and in 1999 he was inducted into the Colorado High School Coaches Association Hall of Fame. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: H.R. Phillips
wordpress.contentText: H.R. Phillips Class of Pre-1990 H.R. Phillips began his service to the nation by entering the United States Air Force, where he attended cadet pre‑flight school at Kessler AFB in Biloxi, Mississippi. He later served with the 9th Air Force as a mechanic in Wiesbaden, Germany, home of the Air Force headquarters. In March 1955, H.R. and his wife moved to Sterling, Colorado, where he became the County Recreation Director. In 1962 the family relocated to Fort Collins, where he was hired as the city’s first Director of Recreation. Four years later, in 1966, he was appointed the first Parks & Recreation Director for the City of Fort Collins. H.R. retired in 1988 after twenty‑six years of dedicated service to the Fort Collins community. His leadership helped shape the city’s early recreation programs and laid the foundation for generations of residents to enjoy quality park and recreation services. An avid outdoorsman, H.R. enjoyed skiing, canoeing, whitewater rafting, horse‑pack trips, camping, and all forms of fishing and hunting. He especially cherished any activity that brought him into the mountains he loved so deeply. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Harry Wise
wordpress.contentText: Harry Wise Class of 1992 Harry Wise was renowned for maintaining his baseball field in the Denver suburb of Englewood with unmatched care and precision. A coaching legend at both the high school and American Legion levels, he built a lasting legacy over four decades in Colorado. The field at Englewood High School, fittingly named Harry Wise Field, stands as an enduring tribute to his impact on the game. For 41 years, Wise coached at Englewood High School, compiling more than 500 wins and guiding his teams to consistent league and state postseason appearances. His 1960 squad finished as the State Tournament runner-up to eventual champion Denver South. For many years, Harry Wise Field also hosted the Colorado Baseball Coaches All State Games, with Wise serving as the sole groundskeeper throughout that entire stretch. Wise’s coaching influence extended beyond high school baseball. He managed the Winner baseball club in the Basin League, one of the premier college summer leagues during the 1960s and 1970s. The league regularly featured top collegiate players, including future major leaguers and World Series pitchers Jim Lonborg and Jim Palmer—both teammates of Wise when he served as the club’s closer. As a player, Wise starred for Colorado State College (now UNC), helping lead the 1949 team to the NCAA Regionals. He earned a win over Southern California in Game Two of the three-game series and exited Game Three with a seven–zero lead before the Bears fell 8–7. His outstanding season earned him All-America honors in 1949. Wise’s legacy lives on through the generations of players he coached, including his son Mike, who pitched for him in the early 1970s. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Howard Dunbar
wordpress.contentText: Howard Dunbar Class of 1997 The late Howard Dunbar began his officiating career while serving in the U.S. Air Force, then known as the Army Air Corps, shortly after World War II. During this time, he acted as Sports Commissioner for baseball, softball, basketball, and volleyball for U.S. personnel stationed overseas. After transferring to Warren Air Force Base in Cheyenne, Dunbar continued to officiate high school sports throughout Wyoming and also worked games in Nebraska and Colorado. He later advanced to football officiating in the Western Athletic Conference, further expanding his influence across the region. Dunbar’s officiating career spanned fifty‑nine years. He worked softball games as early as 1934—earning fifty cents per contest during the sport’s second year—and played a key role in founding the Eastern Wyoming Sports Officials Association in 1980. His dedication to athletics and community service was recognized in 1994 when the Cheyenne City Council named Dunbar Field in his honor, citing his “outstanding citizenship and contributions of time, energy and spirit” to local sports. Howard Dunbar passed away on December 30, 1993. He is survived by his wife, Gwen, and their three children—two daughters and one son. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Howard Wentz
wordpress.contentText: Howard Wentz Class of 1997 Howard Wentz began his umpiring career in 1963 while serving in the Army and stationed in Germany. He discovered a lifelong passion for officiating during those early assignments and continued to develop his craft for more than two decades. He retired after twenty‑one years, noting with humor that while his mind still urged him to drop into position behind the catcher, his knees had begun to disagree. Throughout his career, Howard officiated a wide range of programs, including Little League baseball at Fitzsimons Army Hospital, Girls Fastpitch Softball, American Legion Baseball, and baseball in the Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference. His involvement with the Rocky Mountain Umpire’s Association connected him with prominent figures such as Julie Carabello and David Letofsky, whose example showed him how disciplined mechanics earned the respect of coaches. Howard also worked games in front of legendary Colorado coaches, including John DeSiato, Terry Schiessler, and the fiery Harry Wise—who, as Howard fondly recalled, “was noted for eating umpires alive.” Howard’s dedication to the profession extended to his family, inspiring his oldest son to pursue umpiring at the high school and college levels in Washington State. Even in retirement, Howard enjoyed watching the professionals work—and occasionally miss—a call, a reminder of his belief that “to err is human, to sell it is divine.” Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Irv Brown
wordpress.contentText: Irv Brown Class of 2009 There are few figures in Colorado sports quite like Irv Brown. A former high school and college coach, official, broadcaster, and mentor, Irv built a reputation as one of the most knowledgeable and influential sports personalities in the state’s history. He and his wife, Pat, reside in Broomfield, Colorado. Irv graduated from North High School in Denver and later earned his degree from the University of Northern Colorado. He coached for 25 years across multiple schools and levels, including Metropolitan State College and the University of Colorado. He served as Commissioner of the former Colorado Athletic Conference and as Supervisor of Officials for three collegiate conferences. Even as his career expanded into broadcasting and administration, Irv never lost sight of his roots in high school athletics. While coaching three sports at Arvada High School, his 1964 baseball team won the State Championship, losing only seven games that season. His contributions to Colorado sports led to his induction into the Colorado Sports Hall of Fame in 2001, and Sports Illustrated ranked him 39th among Colorado’s 50 Greatest Sports Figures for his officiating achievements. Irv was also regarded nationally as one of the premier basketball officials of his time, working six NCAA Final Fours. Just a few years ago, he was recognized among the 20 most influential officials in the country. In 1995, he received the CHSAA Distinguished Service Award for his outstanding promotion of high school sports. Beyond the court and field, Irv became widely known for his broadcast work. He hosted a long‑running sports talk show with partner Joe Williams on KKFN Radio (950 AM) from 2–6 p.m. daily. He also served as a youth baseball consultant for several of Denver’s professional sports organizations, conducting clinics statewide. Irv continued to broadcast games across multiple sports for various networks and local cable stations, and he played a key role in mentoring the next generation of sports media talent. A highlight of his broadcasting legacy includes serving on the Air Force Academy football broadcast team. The Colorado Rockies honored him with their Pioneer Award for his contributions to the sport. Notably, Irv also broadcast the first taped ESPN event and the first live ESPN event in the network’s history—both football games during ESPN’s earliest days. Irv Brown’s impact on officiating, coaching, broadcasting, and sports leadership in Colorado is unmatched. His work shaped athletics at every level, and his influence continues to be felt statewide. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Irv Moss
wordpress.contentText: Irv Moss Class of 1990 1990 Hall of Fame Inductee Irv Moss – Denver Irv Moss, who was a sports writer at The Denver Post for nearly half a century, saw virtually every side of sports from youth baseball to high school to college and the pros. A graduate of Denver West High School (noted in obituary sources), he continued his education at Colorado A&M—now Colorado State University in Fort Collins—and the University of Denver. [baseball-r...erence.com] [mlb.com] He served in the United States Army, where his writing career began in a public information office in Germany. Moss returned to Denver and began working nights at the Denver Post, where he befriended sports editor Chuck Garrity, who offered him a position in the sports department. [statscrew.com] Moss advanced to covering high school sports, the University of Wyoming, and the United States Air Force Academy. He also served as a general‑assignment reporter for Colorado State University and contributed coverage of the University of Colorado. [baseball-r...erence.com] Throughout his career, he covered the Denver Nuggets, Denver Broncos, and Colorado Rockies. He helped launch the Denver vs. Suburban All‑Star Baseball Game, originally held at Bears Stadium . [baseball-r...erence.com] Moss described Eric Young’s leadoff home run in the first inning of the Rockies’ inaugural home game at Mile High Stadium as one of the most electrifying sports moments he ever experienced. [baseball-r...erence.com] He also spoke fondly of players who crossed his path, including Goose Gossage, Fred Steinmark , and Don Sessions . [baseball-r...erence.com] , [chsbua.com] Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Jack Smith
wordpress.contentText: Jack Smith Class of 2011 Jack Smith was born on October 17, 1935, in Coaldale, Colorado, and now resides in Rifle, Colorado, with his wife, Barbara. He graduated from Florence High School in 1953 and went on to earn his B.A. from Western State College in Gunnison in 1960. Jack’s career in education and athletics spans decades and includes an extraordinary list of accomplishments. He taught in the Rifle School District for 27 years and coached throughout that entire period—often wearing many hats. Jack led the Rifle High School football team to State Championships in both 1961 and 1973. He coached boys’ and girls’ basketball, guiding the girls’ team to a 2nd‑place State finish in 1986. His baseball teams captured State Championships in 1981 and 1985. Alongside these achievements, Jack also coached track and field, contributing to the development of student‑athletes across multiple sports. Jack’s service to Colorado baseball extended beyond coaching. He spent 20 years as a member of the Colorado State Umpires and worked both Regional and State Playoff assignments, including a 4A Western Slope All‑Star Game. Along with Gordon Cooper and Glenn Curns, Jack helped establish more than 16 umpire associations—a legacy that strengthened officiating across the region. Jack Smith’s long commitment to coaching, teaching, and officiating has left a deep and lasting impact on the Western Slope and throughout Colorado athletics. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: James Roorda
wordpress.contentText: James Roorda Class of Pre-1990 James “Jim” Roorda graduated in 1959 from Denver South High School, where he was a standout athlete and earned All-State honors as a catcher. He continued his education by completing a bachelor’s degree at U.N.C. and later a master’s degree at C.S.U. Jim dedicated his career to vocational education, beginning as a distributive education teacher in Las Animas and Loveland. He later became an associate director of vocational education at Aims Community College during its early years, helping shape its foundational programs. His leadership led to service on the state board for vocational education in Denver, and he concluded his career with the St. Vrain Valley School District. A devoted father, Jim was a constant presence in his sons’ activities—coaching, cheering, and supporting them at every turn. He was also one of five community leaders, along with several key city council members, who played an instrumental role in planning and developing the Centennial Baseball fields. Jim took great pride in this project, knowing it would positively impact thousands of young athletes for generations to come. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Jim Darden
wordpress.contentText: Jim Darden Class of 1994 Jim Darden built an impressive athletic and coaching legacy as a standout guard for the original Denver Nuggets and as a longtime basketball and baseball coach at the Colorado School of Mines. His path might have looked very different. At age seventeen, after helping win the Wyoming state baseball championship and earning All‑District honors as a third baseman, he was offered the chance to sign with the Cleveland Indians. His parents, however, insisted he pursue college first. Darden enrolled at the University of Wyoming, where he contributed as a starting guard for much of the 1943 season on the team that went on to win the NCAA championship. Although he did not play in the tournament because he was inducted into the Army infantry during World War II, his early collegiate success set the tone for his athletic career. After his discharge in 1946, he joined the Denver Nuggets of the powerful Amateur Athletic Union, competing against elite teams such as the Phillips 66ers. When professional basketball’s National Basketball League expanded to Denver in 1949, Darden signed on and quickly made his mark. As a high‑scoring guard, he finished as runner‑up for Rookie of the Year to Dolph Schayes. Despite his professional success, he often noted—with characteristic humility—that one of his proudest achievements was playing competitive softball until age fifty. Darden began a thirty‑five‑year coaching career at the Colorado School of Mines in 1954, becoming a fixture in Golden. He entered the 1988–89 season with 367 career basketball wins, placing him nineteenth on the all‑time NAIA list. His peers recognized his excellence through five Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference Coach of the Year awards and three NAIA District 7 Coach of the Year honors. While Jim Darden’s accomplishments as both player and coach are significant, his induction into the Colorado Sports Hall of Fame celebrates more than wins and statistics. He is remembered as a fierce competitor, a dedicated teacher, and above all, a true gentleman in sport. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Jim Dorsey
wordpress.contentText: Jim Dorsey Class of 1996 Jim Dorsey umpired Colorado high school, American Legion, and college baseball from 1961–1981. His high school career featured fifteen years of postseason assignments and three State Tournaments, complemented by multiple American Legion playoff appearances. In addition to his high school and Legion work, Jim officiated in the Western Athletic Conference, RMAC, and at Denver University. He stepped away from on‑field officiating when his administrative responsibilities at Sheridan High School grew too demanding. Jim served the CHSBUA in numerous roles, including Local Director, Executive Committee member, Assignor, and State President from 1979–1981. He continues to contribute to the game by observing and assessing more than 40 games each season and assigning officials for the Metro League. Jim credits Julius Carabello as the individual who most influenced his umpiring career. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Jim Jenkins
wordpress.contentText: Jim Jenkins Class of 2002 Jim Jenkins was born and raised in Westminster, Colorado, and graduated from Westminster High School in 1959. He continued his education at Colorado State University, where he prepared for a career dedicated to teaching and coaching young athletes. After graduating from CSU, Jenkins began his professional career at Ranum High School. He served as the school’s head baseball coach from 1968–1982 and built one of the most successful programs in the region. Over nineteen seasons, he compiled an impressive 255–76–2 record. His teams captured ten Skyline League championships, won one state championship, and earned second‑ and third‑place finishes in the State Tournament. He was selected Skyline Coach of the Year four times and later honored as both District and State Coach of the Year. Jenkins was known for his integrity, calm leadership, and deep respect for the game. He treated players with fairness, rarely raised his voice, and focused on teaching rather than criticizing. A dedicated student of baseball, he embraced new strategies and prepared meticulously. His creative approach—including a well‑timed double squeeze and a six‑straight‑bunt sequence in a pivotal playoff game—helped his teams outthink and outperform more naturally gifted opponents. Many of Jenkins’s players went on to compete in college, and several reached the professional level. His influence extended far beyond wins and championships; he modeled sportsmanship, preparation, and lifelong respect for baseball. Jim Jenkins was a true credit to high school athletics and is most deserving of induction into the CHSBUA. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Jim Paronto
wordpress.contentText: Jim Paronto Class of 2024 Jim Paronto built a strong reputation as a football player and coach before becoming one of Colorado’s premier umpires. A standout multi-sport athlete, Jim played quarterback at Adams State University, where he earned Academic All-Conference, Academic All-American, and All-Conference honors while also competing on the baseball team. He graduated in 1966 after serving as team captain his senior year and later returned to his alma mater as head coach for both football and baseball. His coaching and officiating career began at Central Union High School in California before he returned to Alamosa, Colorado, to teach and coach at Monte Vista High School. Jim soon advanced into the collegiate ranks with coaching roles at Adams State and Brigham Young University—where he helped the Cougars win a Division I National Championship and earned a doctorate in athletic administration—followed by positions at Oregon State and Colorado Mesa University. At CMU, he led the Mavericks to the NAIA football championship game in his first season. Alongside his accomplishments in education and coaching, Jim established himself as one of the state’s most respected umpires. He served as CHSBUA President from 1999 to 2001 and co-directed the CHSBUA Umpire Training, Evaluation, and Promotion (UTEP) program. He was also instrumental in founding the Mile High Advanced Umpire Clinic, where he trained more than 200 high school umpires over the course of a decade. Jim’s umpiring career was as extensive as it was distinguished. He worked countless league, regional, and state championships and became a fixture at the AABC Connie Mack World Series in Farmington, New Mexico. At the collegiate level, he represented CHSBUA in the Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference and Scenic West Athletic Conference as both an umpire and supervisor while also officiating in the WAC, MWC, and West Coast Conference. He later served as a coordinator in the MWC and held the role of NCAA Secretary-Rules Editor for 12 years—the only CHSBUA umpire ever to serve in a national NCAA rules leadership capacity. A lifelong educator, coach, mentor, and leader, Jim Paronto stands as an exemplary figure whose career has left an enduring mark on Colorado high school and collegiate baseball. His induction into the Joe Rossi CHSBUA Hall of Fame is a fitting tribute to his unwavering dedication and excellence in sport. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Jim Schaefer
wordpress.contentText: Jim Schaefer Class of 2004 Jim Schaefer was born and raised in the farmlands of Nebraska. He and his wife, Arla, moved to Colorado in 1968 and later settled in Frederick, where they planned to retire. A retired registered nurse, Arla often helped him manage the aches and pains that came with a long officiating career. Jim believed strongly in staying physically prepared for every sport he officiated and giving student‑athletes his best effort at every level. Many coaches and players came to know him as “Bud Lite,” a nickname earned through his friendship and officiating experiences with Bud Schoepflin. Jim graduated from Bertrand High School in 1963 and attended Kearney State College from 1963–1965. He earned a bachelor’s degree in Mortuary Science and Forensic Anatomy from the University of Minnesota in 1967. He later became Deputy Coroner of Arapahoe County and spent twelve years as part of the National Transportation Safety Board crash investigation team. Jim also worked twenty‑five years with Lockheed Martin, serving as the Lead for Engineering Administration for the company’s Space Systems division. Throughout his officiating career, Jim contributed to baseball, football, and basketball across the state. He served as a state officer for both the baseball umpires association and the Colorado Football Officials Association, as well as the Colorado High School Basketball Officials. His work with American Legion State Tournaments took him across Colorado, where he addressed dignitaries before tournament play, umpired games, and evaluated fellow officials. Jim’s accomplishments include approximately eighteen State High School Tournaments across all classifications, along with numerous American Legion and Connie Mack State and regional tournaments. He appeared five times at the Connie Mack World Series in Farmington, New Mexico. At the collegiate level, he spent eighteen years in the RMAC, six years in the Mountain West Conference, and eleven years in the WAC, working multiple conference tournaments in California and Hawaii. His postseason résumé also includes the JUCO World Series in Grand Junction and the 1998 NCAA Division II World Series in Montgomery, Alabama. Jim and Arla were married on August 27, 1966, in Nebraska. They raised three children and enjoy a growing number of grandchildren, along with many lifelong friends. Although he retired from officiating for work, physical, and personal reasons, Jim remained active in the community by evaluating officials and supporting the next generation of umpires. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Joe Bellich
wordpress.contentText: Joe Bellich Class of Pre-1990 Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Joe Bonacquista
wordpress.contentText: Joe Bonacquista Class of 2023 Joe Bonacquista was born in 1942—seemingly with a shortstop’s glove on one hand and a ticket to a World Series in the other. While the reality was less instantaneous, it took only twenty years for Joe to make his first College World Series appearance in Omaha as a member of the 1962 University of Northern Colorado Bears. Throughout his youth, Joe’s baseball talents were unmistakable, consistently earning him top hitter awards at nearly every level he played. Decades later, Joe continued to excel on the senior baseball circuit. At age sixty‑nine, he was named Most Valuable Player of his World Series–champion Denver Zephyrs 50+ MSBL team. His remarkable playing career spanned nineteen amateur World Series appearances over forty‑nine years, beginning with the 1962 College World Series and concluding with Senior League World Series competitions in Phoenix and Ft. Myers. As a senior player, Joe captured five national age‑group championships and eight division titles through the Denver Men’s Senior Baseball League and the Roy Hobbs League of Ft. Myers. During his prime playing years, Joe earned team top‑hitter honors five times and was selected to nine all‑star teams. Later in life, he was twice named Senior League MVP, received a Comeback Player of the Year award, and earned the Denver Zephyr Team Spirit Award. Off the field, Joe established himself as a championship‑caliber coach. Over a decade of coaching high school and summer league teams, he led his squads to seventeen championships. He was named South‑Central (High School) League Coach of the Year three times and amassed an impressive 175 high‑school coaching victories—along with sixty additional wins coaching youth baseball. From his first coaching role in Keenesburg, Colorado in 1964 to his final position at Pueblo East High School in 1971, Joe became known as a standard‑bearer of coaching excellence. Three of his former players went on to professional baseball careers. Joe’s legacy extended through his family as well. His son Jeff led the South High Colts to a state championship in 1984 before playing for Wichita State. Together, Joe and Jeff are one of only nine father‑son duos ever to appear in the College World Series, and both are enshrined in the Greater Pueblo Sports Association Hall of Fame. Across Colorado, Joe has been inducted into five halls of fame, from Pueblo to Greeley, recognizing both his athletic and coaching contributions. His impact in the classroom was equally meaningful: he was honored four times with inclusion in Who’s Who Among America’s Teachers during his 33‑year career teaching United States and Colorado History. Joe’s civic and athletic involvement reached far beyond the baseball diamond. He served as a featured clinician at coaching clinics in Southern Colorado, contributed as both a member and president of the Runyon Field Board of Directors, and even took on a change of pace by volunteering as a YMCA basketball coach. wordpress.name: Joe Maciel
wordpress.contentText: Joe Maciel Class of 1998 Joe Maciel joined the CHSBUA when it was formed in 1963 and became one of the first Area Directors for the Colorado Springs group. From the beginning, he set a strong example by assisting new officials and evaluating them during live game situations. In 1968, the Army sent Joe to Europe for three years, where he continued his umpiring career and was selected to work the Army European championship in 1970. He returned to Colorado in 1971 and rejoined the CHSBUA in Colorado Springs. Joe later moved to Grand Junction for two years in 1989 and served as Secretary of that group before returning once again to Colorado Springs. Joe worked State Championship games in 1989 and 1990. His nearly 30‑year umpiring career concluded after knee replacement surgery, but his reputation endured. He was known for his strong baseball sense, steady application of the rules, and consistent hustle on the field. Joe’s go‑forward philosophy and dedication to mentoring officials left a lasting impact on umpiring in Colorado. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Joe Rossi
wordpress.contentText: Joe Rossi Class of 1996 Joe Rossi began his umpiring career in the summer of 1959 and advanced quickly through the ranks. By 1960 he was working high school games, and by 1962 he had moved into college baseball. Over the years, he officiated in numerous high school programs, A Legion, B Legion, Stan Musial, semipro, and college baseball leagues. Throughout his career, Joe worked State Playoffs and State Championship games across every baseball division. He earned statewide respect for his consistent strike zone, becoming best known as one of Colorado’s premier low‑zone umpires. Following his retirement from the U.S. Postal Service, Joe continued to serve the game. He became the CHSBUA Honorary Chairman, contributed to the high school umpire assessment program, and helped organize the CHSBUA at its founding in 1963. Joe’s career reflects a lifetime of dedication to Colorado baseball and the development of future umpires. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Joey Borjon
wordpress.contentText: Joey Borjon Class of 2023 Joey Borjon was a well‑known and beloved figure among students and athletes throughout Pueblo. He served as a security guard at Pueblo Central High School for 30 years and later patrolled the baseball diamond for an additional seven years. His commitment and excellence in his craft earned him a selection to work state championship baseball games for 32 seasons. Joey began his umpiring career in 1983 with the Pueblo study group of the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association (CHSBUA). Over the years, he rose to the position of Pueblo Area Director, where he oversaw umpire recruitment and training. During the off‑season, Joey also umpired high school softball and refereed basketball, demonstrating his versatility and dedication to youth athletics. In the summer months, Joey served as the Training Coordinator for baseball umpires of all ages at the Runyon Field Sports Complex. He also handled assignments for the prestigious Tony Andenucio Memorial Classic. Throughout his career, Joey was an inspiration and mentor to both new and veteran umpires. He was known for his sharp appearance, deep command of the rules and their interpretations, and his calm, expert handling of difficult situations on the field. Coaches and players across Southern Colorado respected his steady demeanor and professionalism. Joey was especially known for the promise he made to coaches at every pregame plate meeting: “We will work hard for your teams this game!” Beyond his impact on athletics, Joey's most cherished role was that of a devoted family man. He is lovingly remembered by his wife, Rita, and their children—Ann, Josh, and Buddy, the latter of whom followed in his father’s footsteps as a high school baseball umpire. Joey also leaves behind his grandchildren: Hannah, Brooklyn, Remi, Avery, and Bostyn. His legacy of leadership, mentorship, dedication, and kindness continues to influence the Pueblo community and the many umpires and athletes whose lives he touched. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: John DeSiato
wordpress.contentText: John DeSiato Class of 1992 John DeSiato has long been a respected figure in the Aurora baseball community, known for shaping some of the finest high school talent in Colorado. A Denver native, he played baseball at Denver North High School and the University of Denver before beginning a coaching career that would leave a lasting impact on the game. John joined Hinkley High School in 1969 as an assistant coach under Terry Schiessler, a former teammate from the University of Denver. He assumed the head coaching role in the early nineteen-seventies and quickly elevated the program, leading the Thunderbirds to Centennial League titles in 1972, 1973, and 1975. His 1973 squad reached the semifinal and finished third, while his 1979 team—driven by a strong junior class and standout Brian Fisher—secured the long-awaited championship breakthrough. Over eleven seasons at Hinkley, John compiled an impressive 140–71–1 record. He coached in the annual Denver Post Suburban vs. Denver All-Star Game in 1972, 1973, and 1975, and in 1979 he was named District 7 Coach of the Year by the American Association of College Baseball Coaches. His coaching career later included guiding future major leaguers Brian Holman and Danny Jackson. John remains a fixture in Aurora, where he continues to serve as the manager of the Aurora Public Schools Stadium. He and his wife, Roseann, are the proud parents of two daughters and one son. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: John Walsh
wordpress.contentText: John Walsh Class of 2012 John P. Walsh was born on November 25, 1943, in Anaconda, Montana. He graduated from Anaconda Central High School in 1962 before continuing his education at Carroll College in Helena, where he earned his bachelor’s degree in 1966. John then attended the University of Montana in Missoula, completing a teaching certificate in 1967, followed by one year of study at Gonzaga School of Law in Spokane, Washington. John’s baseball umpiring career spanned from 1971 to 1999 in Colorado, during which he officiated state tournaments across all classifications. His postseason résumé includes three Legion C State Tournaments, three Legion A State Tournaments, and two Legion B State Tournaments, along with numerous district tournaments and Summer Legion assignments. In addition to his officiating accomplishments, John played four years of college baseball at Carroll College and spent two seasons playing Semi‑Pro baseball in southwestern Montana. John also made significant contributions through leadership roles. He served as Area Vice President for four years, President of CFOA from 1999–2000, and Area Director for the Colorado High School Basketball Association for six years. On March 30, 2000, he received the Greeley Baseball Umpires Association Appreciation Honors Award for his years of service and dedication to baseball in northern Colorado. A respected official, educator, and community contributor, John Walsh exemplified professionalism throughout his long and accomplished career. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Joseph Massaro
wordpress.contentText: Joseph Massaro Class of 2006 Joseph L. Massaro, a Centennial High School graduate from the Class of 1963, built a distinguished career in Colorado baseball. He earned his B.A. from Southern Colorado State in 1967 and continued his education at Adams State College, Western State College, and the University of Northern Colorado. His dedication to both learning and teaching shaped a legacy that spanned more than three decades. Joe began his coaching career in 1967 as an assistant baseball coach at Centennial High School. He later served as the Bulldogs’ head coach, leading standout hitters to S‑CL Batting Championships in 1973 and 1978. From 1981–1985, he was an assistant baseball coach at the University of Southern Colorado, before becoming head baseball coach at Central High School from 1986–1999. Under his leadership, Central earned numerous league and city titles, including the 1988 S‑CL and City Championships, the 1989 City Championship, the 1990 Will Rogers League Championship, State Regionals in 1996, and the 1999 S‑CL Championship. Throughout his career, Joe held professional affiliations with the American Baseball Coaches Association, National High School Baseball Coaches Association, and the Colorado High School Baseball Coaches Association. He served on the Pueblo Runyon Field Board of Directors from 1967–1992 and generously contributed to clinics, camps, and facility improvements at Centennial, Central, the University of Southern Colorado, and the Pueblo Runyon Field Complex. He also assisted with Major League Scouting Bureau professional tryout camps. Joe’s career is marked by numerous honors. He was named South‑Central League Coach of the Year in 1988 and received the Boettcher Foundation Teacher Recognition Award in 1992, along with the ABCA Century Club distinction. He was inducted into the Greater Pueblo Sports Hall of Fame in 1995. His 1997 accolades included the ABCA District VII Regional Coach of the Year, the ABCA 30‑Year Achievement Award, and the CHSCA Level 2 Achievement Plaque. He reached the NHSBCA 200 Victory Club in 1999 and was inducted into the Pueblo Centennial High School Athletic Hall of Fame in 2002. That same year, he authored “How It’s Coached – How It’s Called” for the NFHS Officials’ Quarterly , focusing on pitcher pickoff techniques. Alongside his wife, Gail, Joe Massaro has remained a respected figure in Colorado baseball—admired for his teaching, leadership, and unwavering commitment to the sport. Go–Go–Joe! Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Julius Carabello
wordpress.contentText: Julius Carabello Class of 1990 Julius “Julie” Carabello, a 1990 Hall of Fame inductee from Denver, grew up in Curtis Park and attended Sacred Heart Elementary School and Sacred Heart High School, graduating in 1935. He later coached at St. Joseph’s High School before joining the U.S. Navy during World War II, where he coached Navy recreational basketball and baseball teams while stationed in the Philippine Islands. After returning home, he co‑founded the Junior Parochial League, which has provided athletics to Denver’s Catholic elementary schools for nearly 70 years. As director, he introduced girls’ sports, football weight classifications, postseason consolation brackets, and enrollment-based divisions. His energy and leadership helped shape youth sports across Denver. His passion for athletics evolved into umpiring, where he became one of the highest‑rated umpires in the region, working extensively for the University of Colorado ( University of Colorado ), Colorado State University ( Colorado State University ), the U.S. Air Force Academy ( Air Force Academy ), the University of Denver ( University of Denver ), the University of Wyoming ( University of Wyoming ), and the University of Northern Colorado ( University of Northern Colorado ). Beyond college baseball, he umpired for the Boulder Collegians (Boulder Collegians — via their official presence), National Baseball Congress tournaments ( National Baseball Congress ), and the historic Denver Post Tournament at Merchants Park ( Merchants Park History ). He especially cherished his long service at the Junior College World Series in Grand Junction ( JUCO World Series ). He also officiated NCAA Regional Tournaments in Albuquerque and Salt Lake City. Though he was invited to Florida for professional umpire training, family obligations prevented the move, yet he still regularly called Major League exhibition games in Denver, including work for the Denver Bears ( Denver Bears ). He also worked MLB barnstorming “All‑Star” exhibitions that visited Denver in the 1950s. Carabello founded and served as president of the Rocky Mountain Umpires Association, and after multiple Colorado umpire groups merged, he became the first president of CHSBUA (Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association). In 1997, he received the Pioneer Award from the Colorado Rockies (Colorado Rockies), now permanently displayed in the Coors Field press room. For nearly two decades, he also served as an area sub‑scout for the Pittsburgh Pirates (Pittsburgh Pirates) and the New York Mets (New York Mets). Carabello passed away in 2008, but his deep legacy—especially in umpiring—continues to influence baseball across Colorado. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Keith Bailey
wordpress.contentText: Keith Bailey Class of 2016 Keith Bailey is widely regarded as one of the finest umpires CHSBUA has ever produced. His résumé is exceptional—not only as an umpire, but also as a player and administrator. He served as the Denver Area Director and CHSBUA State President from 1991 to 1993. He was a founding member of the Colorado Collegiate Umpire Association, authored the first CHSBUA Umpire Instructional Handbook (the foundation for the Mile‑High Advanced Umpire Clinic), helped draft the proposal for UTEP (Umpire Training, Evaluation, and Promotion), and was a sought‑after speaker at numerous CHSBUA Master Clinics. As a player, Keith’s accomplishments placed him among Colorado’s elite. After a standout prep career at Hinkley High School under coach John DiSiato, he enrolled at the University of Northern Colorado , where he pitched Tom Petroff’s Bears into the NCAA College World Series. He later played professionally for the San Francisco Giants organization and worked as a professional scout for the Chicago Cubs . Keith also excelled as an administrator. He served as the umpire coordinator for both the United States Air Force Academy and the University of Northern Colorado , and he coordinated umpire assignments for the Cherry Creek Diamond Club for many years. A respected representative of CHSBUA , Keith worked nearly every major high school tournament in the state. He umpired CHSBUA postseason play for nine years, including three State Championship Games. He was selected by the American Amateur Baseball Congress (AABC) for numerous postseason events, including the Connie Mack Colorado State Championships, High Plains Regionals, and five consecutive appearances at the Connie Mack World Series in Farmington, New Mexico. At the next level, he worked seven years of Stan Musial postseason play, culminating in an assignment to the Stan Musial World Series in Battle Creek, Michigan. He was named the AABC National Umpire of the Year in 1994. At the collegiate level, Keith’s credentials remain unmatched in Colorado. He worked in top conferences including the Western Athletic Conference (WAC), Mountain West Conference (MWC), Big XII Conference, Pacific‑10 Conference, and the Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference (RMAC). His assignments included the NJCAA World Series, the WAC Championships (1997), the MWC Championships (2002–2011), and 11 consecutive NCAA Division I Regional Tournaments. Keith’s international experience includes Team USA vs. Japan at Mile High Stadium (1984), the USA Olympic Baseball Festival (1995), and Team USA vs. Cuba before a record‑setting crowd of 66,000 at Mile High Stadium (1990). He also worked professionally as a Triple‑A alternate for 13 years and umpired the Colorado Rockies vs. Colorado Springs Sky Sox game in 1996. In addition to his Hall of Fame honor, Keith was inducted into the UNC Athletics Hall of Fame in 1974. He is currently the City of Aurora Parks and Recreation Supervisor for Youth Development. He and his wife, Marlis, have two grown children—Jeff and Korri (John Treaes)—as well as twin grandsons, Josh and Matt, and a granddaughter, Abby. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ken Furman
wordpress.contentText: Ken Furman Class of 1998 Ken Furman stands as one of the most influential leaders in the history of the Colorado High School Baseball Umpire Association. His unmatched depth of knowledge as a coach, umpire, and athletic administrator helped guide the association’s growth and direction for decades. His calm, steady mentorship has shaped the careers of many of the state’s top officials. Throughout his career, Ken served with distinction in numerous executive roles. He was President of the Colorado High School Coaches Association from 1970–71 and also led both the Boulder Valley Coaches Association and the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association from 1981–84. The CHSBUA Board appointed him Colorado High School Baseball Rules Interpreter from 1989–1997, and he completed two terms as Area Director for the Boulder/Longmont study group. Ken’s officiating accomplishments set a benchmark for excellence. He was selected to work 16 Colorado State High School basketball tournaments and officiated the 1986 NCAA Division I Regional Final. In baseball, he was a fixture at the Colorado High School Baseball Championships for 15 years and was a charter member of the Colorado Collegiate Umpire Association. As an educator, Ken was deeply active in student leadership and athletic administration. He dedicated 29 years to the NBA, CEA, and Boulder Valley Education Association, and was a member of Phi Delta Kappa. He was recognized in the 1956 edition of Who’s Who in American Universities and Colleges as an undergraduate at the University of Denver, where he earned his BA. He later completed his MS at the University of Colorado in 1976 and earned a Type D Administrator Certificate. Tom Smith, CHSBUA Past President, captured Ken’s legacy best: his leadership and involvement have profoundly shaped CHSAA and its programs, positively influencing thousands of students and adults. In a fitting capstone to his career of service, Ken Furman was inducted into the CHSAA Hall of Fame earlier this year. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Lee Rosa
wordpress.contentText: Lee Rosa Class of 2016 As an Athletic Director, Lee was known as the umpire’s best friend—always ensuring they were cared for and protected. He has dedicated 64 years to supporting Aurora athletics, leaving an extraordinary legacy of service. Lee’s career in education began in 1953 as a Physical Education teacher at Crawford Elementary in Aurora. In 1957, he moved to Aurora Junior High School , where he coached football. In 1961, he joined Aurora High School , coaching golf and eventually becoming the school’s Athletic Director. During the summers, he also coached Legion Baseball. A gifted official, Lee was a distinguished football and basketball referee. In 1975, he was hired by CHSAA as an umpire observer and evaluator for postseason assignments in baseball, football, and basketball. His athletic talent extended beyond officiating—he was signed by the Pittsburgh Pirates in 1955 as a third baseman. As an avid golfer, Lee proudly claims to have made 10 holes‑in‑one, though none were caught on camera. He was previously inducted into the Aurora Central Hall of Fame and remains a highly respected supporter of high school athletics and sports officials across all sports. His deep background, extensive officiating knowledge, and warm, engaging spirit have made him a beloved leader throughout the Aurora community. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Leo Bahl
wordpress.contentText: Leo Bahl Class of 1990 Leo Bahl, a 1990 Hall of Fame inductee from Aurora, Colorado ( City of Aurora ), was known affectionately as “Bozo,” the “Clown Prince” of umpires, and was remembered as both a colorful personality and a highly respected official. Born on Christmas Day in 1916 and passing away in 2006, he served in World War II before spending many years working as a postal employee in the Aurora area. From the 1940s through the 1990s, he officiated numerous sports, though baseball became his signature field as a high school and college umpire. His contemporaries included CDCCA Hall of Famers Julie Carbello, Joe May, Dan Weikle, Joe Rossi, Dave Baker, Bud Schoepflin, Dave Letofsky, and fellow 2023 inductee Keith Bailey, along with others such as Bob Blaser, Ken Furman, Wiley Chance, Bob Westoff, Al Raglin, and Bob Finney. For decades he was regarded as a premier big‑game official in the Denver metro area, working countless CHSAA state championships and summer legion tournaments. He was a favorite of Mert Letofsky and the Aurora Legion program, and fellow Hall of Famer Irv Brown often considered him a regular presence at the University of Colorado ( University of Colorado ). Bahl was also a familiar figure at the Colorado School of Mines ( Colorado School of Mines ), where he counted head coach Jim Darden among his closest friends. In addition to baseball, he served for many years as an All‑Star Wrestling official, even bringing some of his wrestling showmanship onto the baseball field. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Leonard Varriano
wordpress.contentText: Leonard Varriano Class of 2012 Leonard “Len” Varriano was born on September 22, 1930, in Moorhead, Minnesota. He graduated from Dilworth High School in 1948 and continued his education at Moorhead State College, where he earned his bachelor’s degree in 1954. Len later pursued graduate studies at the University of Northern Colorado in Greeley, completing his master’s degree in 1968. In addition to his work as an umpire, Len was also a dedicated baseball coach. He began his coaching career at Rothsay High School in Minnesota in 1955. After moving to Colorado, he coached at Greeley Central High School and worked with Legion B Baseball programs. As a player, Len competed in four years of college baseball and spent two seasons playing Semi‑Pro Baseball in Minnesota and Canada from 1952 through 1954. Len’s umpiring career spanned from 1965 to 1992 and included numerous postseason assignments. He worked championships at the Class 2A and Class 3A levels—serving ten years in 3A—and officiated Legion B baseball for an additional ten years. His résumé also includes a Legion State Championship in Arvada, Colorado, and playoff assignments across the 1A, 2A, and 3A divisions. Len also contributed significantly off the field, serving as an Area Director for six years. His long‑standing dedication to baseball—as a player, coach, mentor, and official—has left a meaningful and lasting impact on the Colorado baseball community. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Leroy Garzonio
wordpress.contentText: Leroy Garzonio Class of 2000 Leroy Garzonio began his officiating career more than fifty years ago, starting in Illinois in 1948. He married Josephine Campeggio in 1951, and officiating quickly became part of their shared life. Even while stationed in Germany and Korea during his military service, Leroy continued to pursue the avocation he loved. After returning to the United States, Leroy officiated in Michigan, Illinois, Missouri, and eventually Colorado, where he settled in 1975. In Aurora, he worked football, basketball, baseball, and softball at the high school level and with the Aurora Parks & Recreation Department. Known for never turning down an assignment, he attended all officials’ meetings and was respected by assignors for his reliability. Even after having his equipment stolen from his car, he borrowed gear from fellow officials so he could still work his scheduled games. Leroy was admired for his willingness to mentor younger officials, offering guidance on rules and interpretations. His service was recognized by the Aurora Parks & Recreation Department, and in 1999 he received the Pioneer Award in Football from the Colorado Football Officials Association. Outside of officiating, he enjoyed spending hours fishing, often telling his wife he would return when the fish stopped biting—sometimes well after dark. Leroy worked his final game in the fall of 1998 and passed away on December 14, 1999, in Aurora at age eighty‑three after a lengthy illness. He is remembered as an official of singular character—“after he was made, they threw away the mold.” For those who knew him, it is easy to imagine that, with him in Heaven, God’s team is now complete: He has his umpire. Leroy Garzonio will long be missed as both an official and a remarkable human being. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box 2000 Hall of Fame Inductee Leroy Garzonio – Aurora Leroy Garzonio started his officiating career over 50 years ago. He began officiating in Illinois in 1948. He married Josephine Campeggio in 1951, and she knew that it was a part of the marriage vows to accept officiating sports as part of their life style. Leroy was stationed in the service in Germany and Korea, but still kept up his avocation. He later returned to the states and was registered in Michigan, Illinois, Missouri and Colorado where he resided since 1975. In Aurora, Leroy officiated in the sports of football, basketball, baseball and softball, both at the high school level and with the Aurora Parks & Recreation Department. Whenever asked to do a game at any level, he would never turn it down. His dedication to sports was overwhelming in that he attended all meetings for officials and never made excuses for his schedules. All assignors knew him well, and would call on him for makeup games. He even had his equipment stolen from his car, but managed to borrow equipment from other officials to do the games. Leroy was well liked by all, and was more than willing to help younger officials with rules and interpretations. He was honored by the Parks & Recreation Department of Aurora for his outstanding service, and received the Pioneer Award in Football in 1999 from the Colorado Football Officials Association. When Leroy wasn’t officiating, he was in his boat on the water fishing. He always told his wife, Jo, that he would return when the fish stopped biting, sometimes not returning home until the night hours. Jo said she guessed he had another ball game, and that was just an excuse to stay outside. Leroy worked his last ball game in the fall of 1998, and passed away on Dec. 14, 1999, in Aurora, Colorado after a lengthy illness at the age of 83 years. He will be remembered as an official who “after he was made, they threw away the mold.” But now we feel that in Heaven, God has his pitchers, catchers, infielders, outfielders and his sports fans. Now God’s team is complete: He has his umpire! Leroy Garzonio, both as an official and human being, will be missed.

wordpress.name: Lou Nedbalski
wordpress.contentText: Lou Nedbalski Class of 1994 Lou Nedbalski was born January 9, 1916, in Denver. He married Leila Cheney on January 14, 1940, after the two met at a dance at the old Crystal Hall. They raised a daughter and a son and were later blessed with five grandsons. Known for his sharp wit, Lou was quick with a joke but modest about his accomplishments—especially his acts of bravery as a firefighter. During the 1950s, Lou saved the life of fellow firefighter John Horan’s grandfather during a fire on Larimer Street, entering a collapsed building and carrying him to safety. Over his career Lou earned several nicknames that reflected different chapters of his life: “Ned” during his baseball and basketball days at Manual High School, “Sluggo” when he was a heavyweight boxer, and “Cool Lou” during his years with the Denver Fire Department. A gifted athlete, he often said, “If it’s worth playing, it’s worth winning,” yet he turned down an athletic scholarship to Regis College in order to help support his family. Lou grew up near the stockyards in Globeville, where he learned the value of hard work. He sold newspapers at the Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception and later became a plumber before joining the Denver Fire Department in 1941. Two months later he was drafted into the Army, serving as a firefighter in Italy during World War II. Known as a careful saver, Lou’s thrift became family legend—most memorably when he insisted on reimbursement for a twenty‑five‑cent parking meter fee in 1982, prompting an entertaining official response recognizing “Cool Lou from old Globeville.” Although frugal with himself, Lou was generous with others. His grandson, Terry Nedbalski, recalled that Lou wore the same suit for twenty years and refused to spend money on himself, yet would readily give anything he had to his family. Beyond firefighting, Lou stayed active in sports as a baseball umpire and basketball referee at both the high school and college levels. He also taught himself to play the piano and guitar, reflecting a lifelong curiosity and work ethic that defined his remarkable life. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Manual Boody
wordpress.contentText: Manual Boody Class of Pre-1990 Manual Boody was one of the most respected and admired figures in Colorado high school sports media. For more than 30 years, he passionately covered the prep sports beat for the Rocky Mountain News, becoming a trusted voice for student‑athletes across the state. Guided by an unwavering belief that every athlete deserved recognition, he made it his mission to highlight as many student‑athletes as possible. His sincere interest in the young people he covered—and his commitment to telling their stories—earned him a legendary place within the prep sports community. In addition to his journalistic contributions, Manual proudly served his country in the United States Army, carrying those values of dedication and service throughout his life and career.
