wordpress.name: Mark Denzin
wordpress.contentText: Mark Denzin Class of 2014 Mark Denzin began his umpiring career in 1988 and continued through 1994 with the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association. During that time, he worked both the 3A and 5A Colorado State High School Championships. In 1990 and 1991, he also officiated the prestigious Mayor’s Cup at Denver’s Mile High Stadium. His résumé includes numerous local tournaments such as the Cherry Creek Classic, the Tony Andenucio Memorial, and a wide range of American Legion events. In 1988, Mark was invited to join the Colorado Collegiate Umpire Association, launching what would become an extensive collegiate career. His professional life took him across the western United States. While living in Seattle, Washington, he served as Regional Vice President for the MDA and worked with the Seattle Collegiate Association. He later moved to Chicago, Illinois, where he again served as Regional Vice President for the MDA and became President of the Chicagoland Collegiate Umpires Association from 2002 to 2005. His next stop was Tucson, Arizona, where he served as National Vice President of the MDA and worked with the Arizona Collegiate Umpires Association. Mark now resides in McKinney, Texas, where he serves as President of Ability Connection Texas and continues his involvement in the Texas Collegiate Umpires Association. Mark is widely regarded as one of the most successful CHSBUA umpires on a national level. Following his CHSBUA debut in 1988, he worked numerous postseason assignments and two Colorado State Championships. He also served two terms as a CHSBUA Denver Area Director before his career took him to multiple Western states. At the collegiate level, Mark’s success is unmatched among umpires with Colorado roots. He worked 13 Division I conference tournament games across nine conferences, including the Big East, Mid‑America, Horizon, Big Ten, Missouri Valley, Mountain West, Western Athletic, and Conference USA. His international experience includes seven games involving teams from Korea, Mexico, Canada, and Australia. Mark also served as a fill‑in umpire at the professional level, working in the Pacific Coast League, the Northwest League, and the Midwest League. Additionally, he worked the Northern League (Professional Independent) Championship. Despite his extensive travel and advancement, Mark never forgot his CHSBUA beginnings. He returned to Colorado repeatedly as an instructor for the CHSBUA Mile High Advanced Umpire Clinic and the CHSBUA Master Clinic—continuing to influence and mentor officials across the state. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Melvin Martin
wordpress.contentText: Melvin Martin Class of Pre-1990 Melvin “Mel” Martin began his coaching career in La Jara, serving as head baseball and head basketball coach from 1949–1953. He joined Adams City in 1955 and led the baseball program through 1977, establishing a standard of excellence that shaped the school’s athletic tradition. Under Martin’s leadership, Adams City baseball captured league championships in 1959 and 1962, along with co‑championships in 1960, 1965, and 1966. The 1959 title marked the school’s first league championship in any sport. In 1962, he was selected as head coach for the annual Denver Post Suburban vs. Denver All‑Star Game, guiding the suburban team to its first-ever victory. His achievements culminated in 1977 when the American Association of College Baseball honored him in Miami, Florida, for his long-standing contributions to the sport. Martin was widely respected by his players, many of whom continued their education and played college baseball. Several were inspired to pursue coaching careers, often getting their start in the Little League program he operated for many years. Until his passing in 1981, he dedicated countless hours to youth recreation programs—coordinating activities, coaching, and officiating football, basketball, and baseball. His presence on fields and courts was a constant source of guidance and encouragement. In the classroom, Martin served as chairman of the math department, teaching algebra, math analysis, and computer math. His enduring legacy at Adams City is symbolized by the baseball field that proudly bears his name. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Mert Letofsky
wordpress.contentText: Mert Letofsky Class of Pre-1990 Mert Letofsky was a pioneering force in youth and American Legion Baseball in Aurora. From the moment he first put on a uniform, he proved to be an influential leader whose commitment shaped generations of players. He helped launch Legion Baseball in Aurora in 1951 and remained involved as a coach, administrator, and dedicated supporter for more than 40 years, continuing his service until his passing in 1992. Mert played a key role in establishing the Columbine Rookie League, an original semi‑pro league he founded with friend Bauldie Moschetti. He was also an original founder of Aurora Boys Baseball. Within the American Legion, Mert served in every major capacity, including State Chairman and long‑time tournament director, leading events for 20 years. His coaching career was equally expansive, guiding the Columbine Rookie League for six years, various Legion teams for twenty‑five years, the Denver Freshman League for ten years, Machebeuf, Regis College, and the Aurora Prospectors semi‑pro team. Throughout his remarkable tenure, Mert’s teams secured three state titles and more than 20 league championships. His programs amassed over 1,000 wins, and an impressive 80 percent of his players advanced to compete in college baseball. Many went on to professional careers, while countless others became coaches, extending his influence well beyond the field. Mert was married to his wife, Alice, for more than 40 years and was the proud father of six daughters and three sons, along with many grandchildren. He often said his greatest joy came from watching his sons and former players continue their baseball journeys in college. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Mike Brady
wordpress.contentText: Mike Brady Class of 2009 Michael L. Brady graduated from Fort Collins High School in 1970 and has remained a lifelong resident of the community with his wife, Mala. Following high school, he served in the United States Marine Corps from 1971–1973. Upon returning home, he attended the University of Northern Colorado in Greeley, earning a bachelor’s degree in recreation in 1978. Mike began his umpiring career in 1975 and continued through 2005. He worked NCAA baseball for Bud Schoepflin and Dan Weikle for many years, and he remains active in high school and NCAA softball. Throughout his career, he was selected to umpire numerous District and Regional postseason games, as well as State Championship games across several conferences. His first State Playoff assignment came in 1981, and among his most memorable experiences was a seventeen‑inning semifinal game with Sean Grogan and Jim Kerr that lasted nearly four and a half hours. A respected leader in the officiating community, Mike served as Area Director for the Fort Collins Area Umpires. He led clinics, administered tests for summer ball and high school programs in Fort Collins and Loveland, and served as a clinician at State Master Meetings. He evaluated many umpires over the years and frequently recommended officials for District, Regional, and State Playoff assignments. One of Mike’s most significant contributions was his service to CHSBUA as President Elect, President, and Past President, reflecting his enduring commitment to the advancement of Colorado officiating. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Mike Doohan
wordpress.contentText: Mike Doohan Class of 2023 Mike Doohan often says he “just stumbled across an opportunity.” For the next thirty years, he made the very most of it. His officiating journey began in 1982 when long‑time CHSBUA umpire Gene Crusan recruited him directly from a softball field in Commerce City. The offer was simple—and irresistible: nine‑year‑old softball for eight dollars a game. Like many officials who ultimately build long careers, Mike soon found a home in organized umpiring. He registered with the CHSBUA Boulder group, where he came under the mentorship of respected umpires and leaders Ken Furman and Doug Graham—both Boulder Area Directors and CHSBUA Executive Committee members. With their guidance, Mike quickly developed into a capable and ambitious official. In 1986, four years after his start, Mike attended The Harry Wendelstedt Professional Umpire School, further sharpening his skills. He returned to Boulder and continued what would become a highly successful career. His leadership path soon mirrored his mentors: in the early 1990s, Mike became the CHSBUA Boulder Area Director. He later served on the CHSBUA Executive Committee and built a reputation as a reliable, respected umpire, working postseason games for 25 years, including 15 state tournament contests. Reflecting on his career, Mike says the most important responsibility of an area director is keeping people engaged and positive. His impact showed exactly that. Mike sought out professional‑level training and brought those principles back to his local association. He developed a training system based on sound, fundamental instruction—moving away from the old, anecdotal “tried but not often true” methods of the past. His approach made him one of the first to introduce true professional umpiring concepts to amateur officials, setting a standard that CHSBUA area directors still follow today. Along the way, Mike built lasting relationships with a wide circle of officials. Many of them, in turn, got to know not just a colleague but a mentor—one who took meaningful steps to help others grow both on and off the field. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Mike Kronkright
wordpress.contentText: Mike Kronkright Class of 1992 Myron Myles “Mike” Kronkright was born on October 4, 1922, in Litchfield, Nebraska. He married Ellen S. Swanson in Loup City and served in the U.S. Marines during World War II, with deployments in the South Pacific, New Caledonia, and Guadalcanal. He also worked alongside the Navajo Code Talkers during his service. Mike and his family moved to western Colorado in 1950, where his lifelong love of sports soon led him into officiating. Mike joined the newly formed Grand Junction Football Officials Association, beginning his football officiating career in 1956. He worked high school games throughout the region, officiated at Mesa Junior College, and later covered Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference contests when Mesa became a four‑year institution. His dedication quickly expanded beyond football as he became a respected multi‑sport official. In 1958, Mike began umpiring baseball, working both high school and college schedules. He served on numerous CHSAA postseason crews, including 10 State Championship games and three all‑state games. He also officiated the Junior College World Series for two years and was an alternate several more times. As an accomplished basketball referee, Mike worked 27 consecutive Gunnison Valley League tournaments and multiple State Tournaments, including four championship games and two all‑star games. Widely regarded as one of Colorado’s elite officials, Mike was especially admired for mentoring younger officials. His commitment to training helped elevate the statewide reputation for quality officiating. The National Association for Basketball Officials later adopted a pregame conference sheet he developed in the 1970s—an honor he considered one of the most meaningful of his career. Mike retired from on‑court and on‑field duties in 1986 but remained active by assigning football and basketball officials for area schools. In 1988, the Grand Junction City Council renamed the Pomona Park softball fields the Kronkright Softball Complex in recognition of his service. Over his career, he officiated roughly 1,000 varsity basketball games, 600 varsity football games, and a similar number of baseball and softball contests. Even after “retirement,” he continued umpiring softball twice a week, sustaining the passion for sports that defined his life. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Mitchell Burns
wordpress.contentText: Mitchell Burns Class of Pre-1990 T. Mitchell Burns Jr. was a distinguished presence in Colorado athletics, remembered for his unwavering respect for sportsmanship and his remarkable longevity as an umpire. For generations of high school athletes, he became known as “the oldest umpire they’d ever seen,” a title he carried with dignity and a quiet love for the game. In the spring of 1972, before a freshman baseball game at JFK High School , Coach Dennis Sullivan introduced Burns to his players with a mix of admiration and humor—an early sign that Burns’s legacy extended far beyond his years on the field. Those who met him often felt they were encountering more than a veteran official; they were meeting a piece of Colorado sports history. Born June 14, 1901, in Denver, Burns graduated from the University of Denver and married Helen Hudson on his birthday in 1929. He was elected to the Colorado House of Representatives in 1932 and later operated T. Mitchell Burns Insurance Agents, retiring in 1980 after decades of service to his community. Burns’s passion for athletics was extraordinary. A 1974 Denver Post feature outlined his remarkable devotion: he umpired more than 5,000 games at various levels beginning in 1920; attended every national AAU basketball tournament for three decades; held season tickets to University of Denver football for 40 years; and secured Denver Broncos season ticket priority number eight. He also attended every World Series for 32 years and witnessed 20 MLB All-Star Games. In one memorable October day in 1959, he attended an Air Force football game in Denver, flew to Buffalo for a minor league championship, watched an NHL exhibition that night, and then traveled to New York for the next day’s World Series opener. Burns passed away on October 25, 1985, at age eighty‑four. His obituary in the Rocky Mountain News fittingly opened with the description “long-time baseball umpire and sports enthusiast in the Denver area.” His legacy endures in the stories, memories, and spirit of amateur sports across Colorado. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Myran Dunker
wordpress.contentText: Myran Dunker Class of 2018 Myran Dunker was born in Missoula, Montana, and raised in Akron, Colorado, where he graduated from high school. He went on to serve honorably in the United States Air Force from 1980 to 2000, with assignments that brought him to Buckley ANG Base in Aurora and later to Peterson AFB in Colorado Springs. Myran began his officiating career early, starting with basketball in 1976 and adding baseball in 1988. Over the years, he earned the opportunity to officiate three State Final Baseball Games, a testament to his skill and professionalism on the field. Beyond his on‑field accomplishments, Myran made significant contributions to the CHSAA and CHSBUA baseball programs. He served as the Colorado Springs Area Director from 1996 to 2004 and later as CHSBUA President from 2005 to 2007. He concluded his formal leadership roles as President of the Colorado Springs Umpires Association from 2011 to 2016. One of Myran’s most impactful achievements was his revision of the Colorado Springs Training Program. He transformed it into a more functional, structured, and educational experience for new umpires. His work laid the foundation for what has become one of the strongest and most respected training programs in Colorado. Today, CHSBUA’s New Umpire Training continues to follow the model he built. Myran also established an evaluation system that utilized senior umpires to support growth and consistency across the region. Through decades of service, leadership, and mentorship, Myran Dunker helped shape the development of countless officials and left a lasting mark on Colorado high school baseball. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Neil Devlin
wordpress.contentText: Neil Devlin Class of 2018 Neil Devlin is a native of Phoenixville, Pennsylvania, a suburb on the northwest side of Philadelphia. He attended St. Ann’s School in Phoenixville and later graduated from St. Pius X High School in Pottstown, Pennsylvania. Neil’s newspaper and media career is extensive. He began at The Evening Phoenix (1977–1980) and then worked for the Aurora Sentinel , Aurora Sun , and the Associated Press (1980–1982). From 1982 to 1990, he contributed to The Denver Post as a stringer for the Neighbors sections, covering high school sports. His responsibilities included desk copy editing, layout, and wire handling, as well as supporting coverage of colleges and professional sports. From 1990 to 2016, Neil served as the prep sports editor for The Denver Post , covering more than 300 Colorado high schools. His work included game stories, blogs, columns, features, news reports, and notes. In addition to his writing, he managed budgets, oversaw photo and video assignments, spoke regularly to high school classes, and played an active role in web content design. He currently serves as the Sports Information Director at Mullen High School. Neil’s media presence extends beyond print. He has worked with and appeared on Channel 9 and Channel 4 , and has contributed to Mile High Sports Magazine , the Colorado Preps website, the Boulder Daily Camera , Prep Baseball Report–Colorado , and WoodyPaige.com . He has also made hundreds of radio, webcast, and podcast appearances. Neil is married to Holly, a six‑time swim champion at Cherry Creek who now works in the library at Rangeview High School in Aurora. They have two sons. Ryan, 34, is a Special Olympian and a graduate of Gateway High School in Aurora. Rory, 29, served as a U.S. Army Captain and JAG Officer in the Reserves and National Guard, including service outside Kabul with Special Forces. He is a graduate of Smoky Hill High School in Aurora, Creighton University, and the University of Denver Law School. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Nick Vitale
wordpress.contentText: Nick Vitale Class of 1993 Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Pat Haggerty
wordpress.contentText: Pat Haggerty Class of 2002 Pat Haggerty was born in June 1927 in Denver, Colorado, and lived until December 1994. A proud graduate of North High School, he served briefly in the Navy before attending Greeley for three years. He later signed a professional baseball contract with the Detroit organization and won the 1950 batting title with the Toledo Mud Hens in the Southern Association. He finished his playing career with the Denver Bears. During baseball off‑seasons, Pat began officiating and quickly became respected across multiple sports. He worked high school and college basketball, officiating numerous State Tournaments, and refereed high school and college football in the Skyline, Big Eight, and WAC conferences. His football career reached the national stage with an assignment to the Orange Bowl. Pat joined the NFL in 1962 and served as the Referee in three Super Bowls. At the time of his retirement, he held the distinction of having officiated the most seasons as an NFL referee and was widely regarded as one of the country’s finest officials in both basketball and football. Pat returned to education and athletics as the first baseball coach at Abraham Lincoln High School, where he taught and coached until retiring in 1990. He guided the Lancers to three city championships and two State Championship runner‑up finishes, establishing a lasting foundation for the program. A respected leader of young people and a gifted speaker, Pat often shared insights from his long and accomplished officiating career. His impact on Colorado athletics endures, and he is also honored in the Denver Public Schools Sports Hall of Fame. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Paul Babkiewich
wordpress.contentText: Paul Babkiewich Class of 2015 Paul loved the game of baseball. He played at Wheatridge High School, where he was a three‑time letterman and team captain, graduating in 1974. He went on to attend Colorado State University on a baseball scholarship, graduating in 1978. Paul was a team captain during both his junior and senior seasons. He often joked that he graduated “with honors and achievement,” though those who knew him might smile at that claim. Paul married his college sweetheart, Heidi, and the two shared 35 years together. He was a devoted husband, father to three children—Brittany, Brandon, and Tyler—and grandfather to Sophia and Easton. After college, Paul began a long and successful career with Gallo Wines . He remained in the industry for 36 years, spending more than 20 of those years with Republic National Distributing Company , where he rose to the role of Vice President overseeing sales and operations for Colorado’s wine and beer divisions. He was highly respected and well known throughout the industry, both statewide and nationally. Paul became a High School Baseball official in 1997. He knew the game deeply and worked tirelessly to grow, improve, and excel as an umpire. One of his greatest joys was working alongside his brother Frank, and the two took pride in calling games together—always at a high level, always with professionalism, and always while having fun, regardless of the level of play. Paul believed that players and coaches deserved his very best every time he stepped on the field. His knowledge of the game—and of how coaches expected it to be played—made him a trusted and respected presence wherever he worked. Paul was taken from us far too soon. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Pete Butler
wordpress.contentText: Pete Butler Class of Pre-1990 Pete Butler’s name has long been woven into the fabric of athletics at the University of Northern Colorado. A multi‑sport standout at what was then Colorado State Teachers College, he excelled in football and basketball before beginning a coaching career that helped define the institution’s athletic identity for nearly seven decades. Butler served as head coach of both the men’s basketball and baseball programs, becoming one of the most respected figures on campus. His greatest impact came on the baseball field, where he guided the Bears from 1941–1967 and secured 416 victories—the most of any coach in school history. Under his leadership, the program earned nine appearances in the College World Series, a reflection of his strategic skill and steady influence. In 1969, Butler was inducted into the American Baseball Coaches Hall of Fame in recognition of his exceptional contributions to the sport. His legacy is further honored through Butler‑Hancock Hall, the university’s primary athletic facility that bears his name and stands as a testament to his enduring impact. Among his most memorable teams was the 1949 squad, which posted a 17‑3 season and dominated the Rocky Mountain Conference with an 8‑0 record. After advancing to the NCAA Regionals against Southern California, the Bears rallied behind ace Harry Wise to force a decisive third game. Despite holding a 7‑0 lead, UNC fell 8‑7 in a dramatic finish that captured the competitive spirit and resolve Butler inspired in his players. Pete Butler’s career remains a story of character, resilience, and excellence—one that continues to motivate generations of Northern Colorado student‑athletes. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Pete DAmato
wordpress.contentText: Pete DAmato Class of Pre-1990 Pete D’Amato was a longtime baseball coach whose leadership shaped American Legion baseball in Greeley, Colorado throughout the 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s. On the field, he was known for being direct, firm, and vociferous. Away from the diamond, he was quiet, fun, and self‑effacing—never one to seek the spotlight. His sincere concern for his players defined his coaching style and earned the respect of generations of young athletes. D’Amato launched the Greeley High School Wildcat baseball program, guiding it with patience and a commitment to quality. Six decades later, that commitment was honored when the school dedicated its field as the Pete D’Amato Baseball Field, a lasting tribute to his influence. During the late 1940s through the 1960s, a common saying captured his program’s impact: “Pete’s ‘Cats were the farm system for Alvin Garcia’s Greeley Grays,” a reflection of how many of his players advanced within the community’s baseball tradition. Coach D’Amato remained a mentor and hero to his former players throughout his life, leaving a legacy rooted in loyalty, development, and genuine care for people. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Randy Holmen
wordpress.contentText: Randy Holmen Class of 2017 Randy Holmen is a 1972 graduate of Broomfield High School . He earned a B.A. in Business from Southern Colorado State College (now Colorado State University Pueblo ), where he was also a four‑year starter on the baseball team. In 1985, Randy completed a master’s degree at Adams State University , followed by Principal/Superintendent Licensure in 1989 from Western Colorado University in Gunnison. Since 1979, he has served Colorado schools as a teacher, coach, athletic director, and superintendent. He is currently the Superintendent of the Genoa‑Hugo School District . Randy’s umpiring career began in 1976 when he registered with CHSAA and joined the Pueblo Umpire Association. Over the years, he officiated at every level, including youth baseball (Babe Ruth and American Legion), high school baseball, junior college baseball and women’s softball, and both fast‑pitch and slow‑pitch college softball. In 1979, Randy became affiliated with the Southeast Colorado Umpires Association in Lamar. From 1976 to 2010, he worked annually in multiple classifications of District baseball and, for at least 20 of those years, advanced to regional competition. Over his 34‑year officiating career, he handled numerous quarterfinal and semifinal assignments. Randy’s résumé also includes several high‑profile events: the 2004 CHSAA 4A State Championship Game between Broomfield and Niwot; the 2007 Junior Babe Ruth World Series (13–15) in Jamestown, New York; and the 2009 Cal Ripken World Series (10U) in Lamar, Colorado. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ray Belfiore
wordpress.contentText: Ray Belfiore Class of 2025 A lifelong student of the game, Ray Belfiore’s journey behind the plate began immediately after high school, when he took the Colorado high school umpiring test in 1982. In those formative years, he developed his craft alongside the respected Aurora-based crew led by Mert and Dave Letofsky. Starting in sub-varsity high school games, Ray quickly grew his skills through the rigors of the American Amateur Baseball Congress (AABC), calling games in the competitive Connie Mack (18+) and Stan Musial (collegiate) circuits. It wasn’t long before Ray was recognized as a standout new talent. His professionalism, consistency, and dedication earned him numerous championship assignments and set him apart among his peers. Over 30 years, he became a fixture in these elite leagues. In 1987, Ray took a major step forward by attending the Brinkman Professional Umpire School, an experience he credits as transformative. There, he learned what it truly takes to excel in the profession. Upon returning to Colorado, Ray brought that knowledge home, becoming an instructor at the CHSBUA-sponsored Mile High Advanced Umpire Clinic. He became a vital asset to the clinic, training not only aspiring umpires but also the instructors—sharing cutting-edge professional techniques and his personal blueprint for success: Work hard every game, no matter the score. Listen to the veterans. Always be learning. Ray's success continued to climb , as he entered the collegiate officiating ranks with assignments in the Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference (D-II) and major Division I conferences like the Mountain West, Big XII, WAC, and West Coast Conference. Though his high school schedule lessened over time, his passion for mentoring never waned. He remained a pillar of the CHSBUA’s instructional efforts for over two decades, particularly through the UTEP/Mile High Advanced Umpire Clinic and Master Clinics. Ray’s dedication to the game has earned him numerous prestigious assignments and awards: Career Highlights: 2× Connie Mack World Series umpire 2× JUCO World Series umpire 12× Mountain West Championships umpire 10× NCAA Division I Regionals umpire 2× NCAA Division II Regionals umpire AAA alternate umpire (Zephyrs, PCL Sky Sox) – 15+ years Legacy Among Peers Fellow umpire Keith Bailey fondly remembers Ray as “fearless” and “passionate,” known for holding his crew—and himself—to the highest standard. If Ray was on your crew, you were going to have fun—but you were also going to be accountable. He made you better. Giving Back to the Umpiring Community Ray’s career was defined not only by his achievements but also by his relentless desire to give back to the umpiring community. Through 20+ years of clinics, mentorship, and leadership , Ray became the very definition of an “umpire’s umpire.” Ray’s career continues to inspire and uplift the next generation of umpires, a testament to his passion, professionalism, and his unwavering commitment to the craft. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Raymond Powe
wordpress.contentText: Raymond Powe Class of 2002 Raymond Powe enjoyed a distinguished thirty‑four‑year high school officiating career from 1976–1999, making lasting contributions across five sports. In addition to his work on the baseball diamond, he officiated football for 19 years, basketball for 17 years, softball for 13 years, and volleyball for five years. Ray began umpiring youth baseball in 1966 while serving in the U.S. Army in Germany. Over the next decade, he missed two seasons while completing two tours in Vietnam. When he retired from active duty at Fort Carson in 1976, he entered the Colorado high school ranks with eight years of prior experience and quickly became one of the most respected umpires in the Colorado Springs association. Throughout his career, Ray worked district playoffs for ten years and regional playoffs for five years. His excellence earned him three assignments to State Championship games, including his final appearance behind the plate for the 1996 2A title game. Beyond the high school season, he also umpired semi‑pro contests for Athletes in Action in the 1980s and handled games for Colorado College until the program was discontinued under Title IX. Ray Powe’s long service, versatility, and steady presence left a lasting impact on officials and athletes throughout the region. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Rich Czernicki
wordpress.contentText: Rich Czernicki Class of 2008 Richard “Z‑Man” Czernicki, a graduate of Northglenn High School, was a four‑sport athlete in 1972, competing in football, baseball, basketball, and track. He continued his athletic career with five years of college football—two seasons at Mesa Junior College in Grand Junction and three seasons at Western State College in Gunnison. In the fall of 1976, he played in the NAIA National Championship game, where Western faced Texas A&I. He completed his degree at Western, earning a B.A. in Physical Education with a minor in English Literature. Czernicki taught for 28 years at Northglenn High School, retiring in 2005. During his tenure, he coached football for eight years, basketball for eight years, and baseball for two years. He also led the Junior Varsity basketball program, compiling an impressive 105–38 record and winning five league championships. His officiating career brought him widespread recognition across multiple sports. Czernicki spent 23 years umpiring high school baseball, 18 years officiating high school football and basketball, five years in high school girls’ softball, and 11 years in college baseball. He worked five State Championship games, eight semifinals, and accumulated 20 years of State Tournament experience. In 1995, he was named Denver Area Umpire of the Year and served four years as crew chief for the Colorado High School All‑State Games. Czernicki also contributed to the development of new officials. With grant support from School District 12, he created training courses for baseball, softball, and basketball officials, integrating these classes into the intramural program at Northglenn High School for two years. Throughout his career, Czernicki officiated several memorable moments, including a triple play in the 1993 State Final between Lamar and La Junta; the 1995 State Final featuring Arvada West vs. Cherry Creek with Roy Halladay and Darnell McDonald; a 35‑strikeout performance in a 1998 game between Highlands Ranch and Hinkley; four out calls at the plate in the first inning of a Boulder vs. Fairview matchup; and three batter‑interference calls in a single half‑inning during a 2001 Fairview vs. Overland game. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Richard "Dick" Reininger
wordpress.contentText: Richard "Dick" Reininger Class of 2016 - Brighton Originally from Johnstown, Pennsylvania, Richard “Dick” Reininger has served as a distinguished official across a wide range of sports. In addition to umpiring baseball and softball, he has officiated football, basketball, volleyball, and—almost—roller derby. Dick had the honor of working the first-ever game at Jefferson County Stadium as well as the inaugural game at Prairie View High School . He also raised an umpiring family, working the State All‑Star Game twice with his sons, Craig and Travis. For many years, Dick was the primary umpire educator for CHSBUA , leading instruction for new umpires in the Denver area. His teaching efforts extended statewide, with clinics focused heavily on Northern Colorado and even in Aspen—including during ski season. Dick’s commitment to education extended beyond officiating. He taught at Aims Community College and also served the public as a Brighton, Colorado City Councilman for eight years. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Richard Chandler
wordpress.contentText: Richard Chandler Class of 2001 Richard Chandler left his family farm in Yuma to attend Colorado State Teachers College—now UNC—bringing with him a strong work ethic, a pair of tennis shoes, and a reliable jump shot. Soon after graduation, his passion for athletics guided him into officiating, where he exchanged playing gear for a whistle and striped shirt. While teaching provided his primary career, summers still found him back in familiar territory, this time wearing a chest protector, leg guards, and a catcher’s mask. Rich spent thirty‑one years umpiring baseball and softball in the Denver Metro area and quickly earned a reputation as one of the region’s top officials. For twenty years, he was a fixture at the CHSAA State Tournament, working five big‑school (5A–6A) State Championship games, including his final in 1995 with Joe Rossi and rookie Tom Pontarelli. He also worked the 3A State Championship for six consecutive years. In total, Rich officiated eleven CHSAA State High School Baseball Championship games—a tenure with few equals in Colorado umpiring history. Rich extended his talents to the college level, working Western Athletic Conference baseball for seventeen years and Big Eight baseball for eight years. Alongside his officiating achievements, he enjoyed a successful career in education as a teacher, basketball coach, and athletic administrator. He also served as principal for several Jefferson County summer school sessions and taught driver’s education, navigating Denver traffic with steady patience. From 1985–88, Rich served as a Denver Director for the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association (CHSBUA). He later retired as Athletic Director at Standley Lake High School, where he led a restructuring of the athletic department that helped produce JEFFCO championships and state‑ranked programs in baseball and wrestling. He continues to officiate football in the Western Athletic Conference and will serve on the CHSBUA Umpire Evaluation Committee. Rich lives in Golden, Colorado, with his wife, Cynthia. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Richard Fanning
wordpress.contentText: Richard Fanning Class of 2007 Richard “Dick” L. Fanning devoted his life to education and baseball, building an enduring legacy in the Colorado Springs community. He taught for thirty-two years, including thirty within the Air Academy School District, while also serving as a high school baseball coach for sixteen years as a head coach and nine years as an assistant or volunteer. Across high school, Legion A, Connie Mack, and Babe Ruth levels, he amassed more than 800 career victories. In 1982, Dick coached the West Team at the National Sports Festival in Indianapolis. He later scouted for the Milwaukee Brewers from 1993–1999. Known for innovation, he was the first—albeit unofficial—to take a Colorado high school baseball team to Arizona for spring break practice, a move he joked brought him “some heat.” Dick also contributed as a member of the Colorado Springs Umpire Association from 1968–1975 and spent twenty‑five years with the American Baseball Coaches Association (ABCA), where he served six years on both the High School Committee and the National High School All American Committee. Dick earned numerous honors throughout his career. He was named Colorado Springs Baseball Coach of the Year in 1980, 1984, and 1989, and Colorado Baseball Coach of the Year in 1989. That same year, he was recognized as District 6 Coach of the Year by the ABCA, and his team won the State Baseball Championship. In 1992, the Colorado High School Baseball Coaches Association selected him as Mr. Baseball. He was also twice honored by the ABCA as Volunteer Coach of the Year for his summer coaching service. His service to the game extended far beyond the field. Dick served as vice‑president of the Colorado High School Baseball Coaches Association in 1983 and president in 1984. He contributed to the CHSAA Baseball Committee in 1983–84, spoke twice at the Colorado High School Baseball Coaches Clinic, and authored three articles for the Colorado High School Coaches Association. He also spent four years helping select the Top 40 Seniors for the annual Senior All‑Star Game at Coors Field. A 1959 graduate of Shawnee Mission North High School in Kansas, Dick played baseball at the University of Kansas from 1962–1964, earning his B.S. degree. He later completed his master’s at Adams State in 1983. He served three years in the U.S. Army, rising from second lieutenant to captain. Dick and his wife, Susan, have been married for thirty‑nine years and are the proud parents of two children. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box 2007 Hall of Fame Inductee Richard ‘Dick’ L. Fanning – Colorado Springs Dick has kept very busy throughout his life. He taught for 32 years, 30 for the Air Academy School District. He coached high school baseball for 16 years as a head coach, and 9 years as an assistant or volunteer coach. Dick has won over 800 games between high school, Legion A, Connie Mack and Babe Ruth baseball games. In 1982, Dick coached for The National Sports Festival, West Team in Indianapolis, Indiana. He was also a scout for the Milwaukee Brewers from 1993 until 1999. On a light note, Dick unofficially took the first Colorado high school baseball team to Arizona to practice during spring break. He said he “took some heat for that.” Dick was a member of the Colorado Springs Umpire Association from 1968 until 1975 and a 25-year member of the American Baseball Coaches Association. He served as District Chairman for 6 years on the High School Committee of the American Baseball Coaches Association and the National High School Committee of the American Baseball Coaches Association, All American Committee. Dick has many accomplishments and honors. He was Colorado Springs Baseball Coach of the Year 1980, 1984 and 1989. He was Colorado Baseball Coach of the Year 1989 and voted the District 6 Coach of the Year by the American Baseball Coaches Association in 1989. His team won the State Baseball Championship that year as well. Dick was selected as Mr. Baseball 1992 by the Colorado High School Baseball Coaches Association. He was twice voted Volunteer Coach of the Year by the American Baseball Coaches Association for his volunteer efforts coaching his high school team in the summer. Dick’s contributions are also many. He was vice-president of the Colorado H.S. Baseball Coaches Association in 1983 and then President of the association in 1984. He served on the Colorado H.S. Activities Association Baseball Committee in 1983-84. He spoke on two occasions at the Colorado H.S. Baseball Coaches Clinic and written three articles on baseball for the Colorado H.S. Coaches Association. Dick served four years on the committee to select the Top 40 Seniors to play in the Senior All Star Game held annually at Coors Field. Dick attended Shawnee Mission North High School in Shawnee Mission Kansas and graduated in 1959. He played baseball at Kansas University from 1962-64 where he also earned his B.S. Dick eventually went to Adams State in Alamosa, Colorado and earned his Master’s in 1983. He also spent 3 years in the U.S. Army as a 2nd Lieutenant to the Captain. Dick and wife Susan for 39 years now and they have two children.

wordpress.name: Richard Hawkins
wordpress.contentText: Richard Hawkins Class of 2001 Richard “Hawk” Hawkins has lived a life shaped by service, resilience, and a deep love for baseball. A veteran of three major conflicts—World War II, Korea, and Vietnam—he became known not only for his military heroism but also for his decades of excellence as an umpire. While many in the baseball world recognized him in the familiar blue of an official, his fellow servicemen knew him as a highly respected Chief Master Sergeant who served twenty‑eight years in the United States Air Force. Hawkins first entered the military through the United States Army, serving four years as a machine gunner in the European Theatre from D‑Day through V‑E Day. His decision to join the Air Force came during the Battle of the Bulge, when a break in the weather allowed American aircraft to resupply and support ground forces. That moment, which Hawkins credited with saving countless lives, inspired his shift to the “Wild Blue Yonder.” Baseball became part of Hawkins’s life soon after. While stationed at Scott Air Force Base in 1950, he began umpiring in the Clinton County Baseball League. An overseas assignment soon introduced him to military and Japanese college baseball, leading to three appearances in the Far East World Series in 1952. Following training at the George Barr Umpire School in Japan, Hawkins returned to the United States and worked as a reserve umpire in the Florida State League. His assignments took him to California, where he officiated high school, college, and semi‑professional baseball before serving in Okinawa from 1960–64. A highlight of that tour was umpiring a 1961 exhibition between the Detroit Tigers and the Military All‑Stars. After returning stateside, Hawkins continued to excel, earning assignments to CIF Baseball Championships before and after a brief tour in Vietnam. Relocating to Colorado in 1976, he became a respected presence in high school baseball and the Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference, as well as the Men’s Senior Baseball League and local legion programs. In 1992, Hawkins again represented his country on the international stage, umpiring Olympic Baseball when Team USA faced Team Cuba at Denver’s Mile High Stadium before more than 66,000 fans. For more than twenty‑five years, Hawkins worked high school postseason games across nearly every league in the Denver Metro Area. He served as the Denver Director for CHSBUA, contributed as a valued clinician at CHSBUA Master Clinics, and mentored countless new umpires as they navigated their first on‑field challenges. Hawkins remains active as a CHSBUA evaluator and lives in Aurora with his wife, Doris. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Richard Mauro
wordpress.contentText: Richard Mauro Class of 2010 Richard Joseph “Blue Blaze” Mauro graduated from North Denver High School in 1948. He now resides in Littleton, Colorado, with his wife, Marie. Throughout his life, Richard has made extensive contributions to community athletics, earning the respect of players, coaches, and fellow officials across the state. Richard umpired for a total of eighteen years at both the high school and collegiate levels. During that time, he became a member of the Rocky Mountain Umpires Association, where he served as President for two years and spent an additional two years on the Board of Directors. Alongside his umpiring career, Richard also dedicated significant time to coaching. He led the Northside Community Center’s B‑Legion baseball team for five years, guiding them to two League Championships and one State Championship. As Assistant Coach to Ron Mitchell, he helped the Metz Lumber Girls’ Softball Team reach three State Championships and achieve a seventh‑place finish at the National Tournament in San Antonio, Texas. Richard’s umpiring résumé includes multiple high‑profile assignments, such as officiating three All‑Star Games at Bears Stadium. He also represented fellow officials at a C.H.S.A.D. meeting, where he successfully advocated for increased pay for high school baseball umpires. A respected leader, coach, and official, Richard “Blue Blaze” Mauro has left a lasting and meaningful mark on Colorado baseball. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Robert Blaser
wordpress.contentText: Robert Blaser Class of 2007 Robert “Bob” S. Blaser has enjoyed a long and distinguished career in Colorado athletics. A 1972 graduate of Ranum High School in Westminster, he excelled in both baseball and football. He earned all‑conference honors as a shortstop on Ranum’s 19–1 baseball team, which finished third in the State Tournament after a narrow 2–1 semifinal loss to Pueblo Central, the eventual state champion. Bob was also an all‑conference quarterback, playing for his father, Loren Blaser, who served as Ranum’s head football coach from 1961–1974. Bob began his college career at the University of Utah in 1973 before transferring to Mesa College in Grand Junction, where he completed his associate degree in 1974. He later earned a bachelor’s degree in Business Administration from the University of Southern Colorado in 1976. During four seasons of college football—one at Mesa JC and two at Southern Colorado—his athletic highlight came in 1974 when he competed in the JUCO World Series and earned a place on the all‑tournament team. For more than twenty‑one years, Bob has contributed to Colorado high school baseball as an umpire, with nineteen seasons including postseason assignments. His accomplishments include officiating four State Championship games, most recently the 1995 title contest between Cherry Creek and Arvada West, featuring standout players Roy Halladay and Darnell McDonald. Bob also served two years as Area Director for the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association. Beyond the field, Bob has built a successful career as a sales representative with Denver Athletic Supply. He and his wife, Debbie—formerly Debbie Monaco and sister of respected umpire John Monaco—have celebrated more than thirty years of marriage. They are the proud parents of four children: Bryan, an assistant baseball coach at Mountain Range High School; Corey, now in his sixth season as a minor‑league umpire and a crew chief in the AA Eastern League; Craig; and their daughter, Deenna. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Robert Campbell
wordpress.contentText: Robert Campbell Class of 2008 Robert “Bus” Campbell was born in Fort Morgan and moved to Denver at age eight. He passed away at age eighty‑seven and was survived by his wife, Helen; daughters Patricia, Cindy, and Sandy; and son Gary. Another son, Randy, who coached baseball at Heritage High School, died in 1987. Bus graduated from Denver Manual High School in 1939, lettering in baseball, basketball, and football, and was a member of Manual’s 1938 State Basketball Championship team. From 1942 to 1945, Bus served in the United States Coast Guard. He later attended the University of Denver, earning a bachelor’s degree in health, physical education, and recreation in 1959. For the next twenty‑two years, he worked as a physical education teacher in Littleton Public Schools, earning Littleton Public Servant of the Year honors in 1970. During this time, he also built a dual career as a major league scout and pitching coach, working with high schools, colleges, American Legion programs, and eventually the baseball program at the University of Colorado. He became a key figure with the highly successful Boulder Collegians semi‑pro team. Bus began scouting in 1951 with the Milwaukee Braves and spent thirty years evaluating talent for five major league organizations. His work opened doors for countless Colorado athletes. While scouting for Toronto, he began working with fourteen‑year‑old Roy Halladay in 1995. The Toronto Blue Jays later selected Halladay in the first round out of Arvada West High School. Their bond endured, with Halladay often calling Bus after games to discuss pitching and gifting him a satellite dish and grandfather clock as gestures of gratitude. As a pitching coach, Bus earned a reputation unmatched in Colorado. Eighty‑two of his pupils went on to pitch professionally, including Roy Halladay, Burt Hooton, Mark Huismann, Scott Elarton, Jamie Moyer, Brian Fisher, Bob Welch, Brad Lidge, Danny Jackson, Ed Lynch, Craig Lefferts, Tim Lollar, Jay Howell, Mark Knudson, Mike Trujillo, Darrel Akerfelds, George Frazier, Clint Zavaras, Steve Busby, Dick Ruthven, and Nick Willhite. In 1985 alone, twenty‑five former Campbell pupils appeared in 731 major league games, winning 107 contests and recording 80 saves. Bus was widely admired not only for his technical expertise but for his character. During a Colorado Springs reception honoring Goose Gossage’s Baseball Hall of Fame induction, Rich “Goose” Gossage paid special tribute to Bus, noting that he “was at the top of the chart as a pitching coach and as a person” and helped extend his career by several years. Though never a head coach and never one to seek attention, Bus left a lasting legacy. Colorado’s remarkable tradition of producing elite pitchers owes much to his quiet dedication, and many former players credit their careers to his guidance. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Robert Schnabel
wordpress.contentText: Robert Schnabel Class of 2010 1996 Hall of Fame inductee Joe Rossi – Arvada Presenter, Bud Schoepflin Joe started umpiring in the summer of 1959 and began doing high school games in 1960 and college in 1962. He umpired in numerous high schools, A and B Legion, Stan Musial, Semi-pro and college baseball games. He worked state playoffs and championship games in every baseball division. Joe was best known as a low zone umpire in the state of Colorado and was able to keep that reputation throughout his career. Now retired from the U.S. Postal Service, he is the CHSBUA Honorary Chairman and active in the high school umpire assessment program and was on the committee to organize the CHSBUA in 1963 Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Roger Kinney
wordpress.contentText: Roger Kinney Class of 1993 Roger Kinney’s lifelong connection to Denver began at birth and grew through a distinguished athletic and administrative career. A standout multi‑sport athlete at East High School, he led the city’s prep baseball league in batting during the early 1950s before continuing his baseball career at the University of Colorado, where he earned his bachelor’s degree in 1960. He later completed a master’s degree in accounting at the University of Denver in 1964. Kinney’s behind‑the‑scenes influence in Colorado sports began in the 1960s as a member of the Metro Sports Committee. After assuming leadership of the committee and becoming president of the Colorado Sports Hall of Fame, he helped spearhead Denver’s pursuit of major national events. Working alongside noted figures such as Vince Boryla, Eddie Crowder, Bill Daniels, President Gerald Ford, Mayor Federico Peña, Johnny Dee, Dick Eicher, Arnie Ferrin and Bob Lee, he guided the city’s successful bids to host NCAA regionals in 1985 and 1989, culminating in Denver welcoming the 1990 “Final Four.” His work with the Metro Sports Committee also played a key role in bringing Major League Baseball to Colorado. Kinney served on the Metro Stadium Authority and was executive director of the Denver Baseball Commission, helping shape the city’s case for expansion. When MLB awarded Denver a franchise in 1991, he became the first employee of the Colorado Rockies, leading the Colorado Rockies Foundation and directing community outreach programs until 2003. Reflecting on his career, Kinney credited his accounting background for opening the door to meaningful service—allowing him to blend business expertise with a passion for sports. His leadership in securing major national events and a Major League franchise left a lasting impact on Denver’s sporting profile, economy and national visibility. As journalist Sam Adams noted, Kinney’s many contributions “pointed toward new opportunities to enhance Colorado’s presence on the national sporting scene.” Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ron Piotraschke
wordpress.contentText: Ron Piotraschke Class of 2002 Ronald Piotraschke graduated from the University of Colorado in 1954 with a Bachelor of Science degree in Physical Education. He began umpiring baseball in Jefferson County with the Umpire’s Association of Colorado under the guidance of Bill Vincent. Ron quickly developed his skills while working midget and junior games in a one‑umpire system, earning four dollars per game and typically handling Saturday and Sunday doubleheaders. As his experience grew, Ron advanced through Legion A and B levels and eventually reached semi‑pro baseball at the Jefferson County Fairgrounds. He also officiated fast‑pitch softball in the Denver and Englewood areas. During his nine years at Cheraw School near La Junta, he helped establish the school’s baseball program. He later spent four years in England before settling in Colorado Springs, where George Theodore and Bob Mullenberg further guided his officiating career. While in Colorado Springs, Ron worked numerous Class A Tournaments, district and regional postseason games, and State Championship contests from 3A through 5A. His college assignments included games at the Air Force Academy and Colorado College. He also played a key role in persuading the Metro League to adopt the three‑umpire system for its league tournament. Ron made a lasting contribution to the Colorado Springs officiating community by serving as the baseball and softball assignor for seventeen years, retiring from that position in 1994. A dedicated educator, he taught in Colorado schools for twenty‑nine years. Ron now resides in Fort Collins with his wife, Alice. They have two children, Mike and Julie, and one grandson, Sam. He continues to work high school softball. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ron Sisson
wordpress.contentText: Ron Sisson Class of Pre-1990 Ron Sisson graduated from Greeley High School before earning his bachelor’s and master’s degrees from Colorado A & M (now Colorado State University). He proudly served two years in the U.S. Army. Ron dedicated 31 years to education, sharing his wisdom and sense of humor with students and colleagues. His career included impactful roles at Windsor High School, Lesher, and the opening of Lincoln. He concluded his service as Principal of Fort Collins High School, where his leadership left a lasting impression on the school community. A lifelong supporter of athletics, Ron’s passion for sports shaped his years as a player, coach, and official in baseball, basketball, and football. He was also a life member of the CSU Alumni Association and the Greater Ram Club. Ron Sisson is remembered for his steady leadership, commitment to students, and devotion to education and athletics throughout northern Colorado. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ross Barlow
wordpress.contentText: Ross Barlow Class of 2015 Ross Barlow was born in Omaha, Nebraska on October 16, 1953. He attended Monte Vista High School - Home , graduating in 1972, and went on to earn a Bachelor’s degree (1979) and an M.A. (1980) from Adams State College in Alamosa, Colorado. Ross resides in Monte Vista, Colorado. Ross enjoyed a long and distinguished officiating career. He worked as a high school umpire for 33 years, beginning in 1977, when he was assigned to the 4A State Championship, Semi‑Final, and Regional rounds. Beginning in 1988, he became a regular presence in postseason high school baseball throughout southern Colorado. From 1989 through 1999, he umpired in 2A, 3A, 4A, and 5A classifications, including multiple State Semi‑Finals and the CHSAA All‑State Games in 1993 and 1996. Ross also spent more than 25 summers working high school summer baseball. In addition, he officiated seven Connie Mack World Series tournaments in Farmington, New Mexico , earning four Championship Plate assignments—an honor reserved for the most trusted officials. Ross’s leadership extended well beyond the field. Over the course of his career, he received numerous assignments and held several important roles, including: Area Director in Monte Vista and Alamosa for 20 years Training instructor for Area 13 umpires in Monte Vista and Alamosa for 20 years Umpire Evaluator for more than 30 years Assignor for high school baseball and basketball in the San Luis Valley for 16 years Through decades of service, Ross helped develop and mentor countless umpires while elevating the quality of officiating throughout southern Colorado. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Ross MacAskill
wordpress.contentText: Ross MacAskill Class of 2003 Ross MacAskill began his umpiring career in 1971 in Virginia while stationed with the USMC. He continued officiating in California from 1973–75 before settling in Colorado Springs, where he worked from 1976–2002. Throughout his career, Ross earned numerous postseason assignments, including the 1980 Babe Ruth Regional World Series in Burlington, Colorado, and the 1983 Olympic Sports Festival in Colorado Springs. He also served as an Area Director for the CHSBUA in 1987–88. Ross was selected to umpire several State Championship games during the late 1980s and again in 1992, 1994, 1997, and 2002. Ross cherished many moments from his three decades on the field: seeing players advance to the Major Leagues, navigating Colorado’s unpredictable spring weather, and working alongside coaches devoted to their athletes. He embraced the transition from an outside to an inside chest protector, enjoyed high school all‑star games at Mile High Stadium and Coors Field, and once even thought tripleheaders were fun. He took great pride in contributing to a game where integrity mattered, striving to give his best so players could give theirs. Ross also valued the camaraderie of the fine men he umpired with and treasured watching his sons grow to love baseball. One of his most meaningful memories was seeing his wife in the stands—quietly supporting him at his first game in 1971 and again at his final game in May 2002. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Sam Corsentino
wordpress.contentText: Sam Corsentino Class of Pre-1990 Sam Corsentino graduated from Lakeside/Vineland High School (now Pueblo County High School) in 1936. During his teenage years, he was an active participant in softball and boxing, laying the foundation for a lifetime devoted to athletics and community service. Over the next decade, Sam competed in local, city, and inter‑city leagues in basketball, baseball, and softball. He spent 10 seasons as the head men’s and women’s softball coach at the Pueblo Army Depot, while also serving five years as an assistant boxing coach and two years as an assistant coach for the 29th Ammunition Squad. His leadership extended into youth baseball, where he was named District IV Commissioner of the Babe Ruth 16/18 Division and later served as Assistant State Commissioner for the Colorado Babe Ruth Baseball Association. Sam also contributed to the broader sports community as a member of the Runyon Field Board of Directors from 1977–79. Beyond athletics, he dedicated 10 years as a volunteer director for Pueblo Diversified Industries, supporting individuals with intellectual disabilities. He also volunteered his time to assist the Special Olympics, reflecting his deep commitment to service. Known as a small man in stature but a giant in integrity, Sam earned respect for his fairness and unwavering impartiality when officiating. He took his role seriously, always calling plays according to the book and standing firm when challenged by players or coaches. In recognition of his many contributions, Sam received a Lifetime Membership in the Greater Pueblo Sports Association in 2008. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Steve Heuer
wordpress.contentText: Steve Heuer Class of 2025 Steve Heuer’s name is synonymous with commitment, endurance, and excellence in Colorado high school officiating. After arriving in Denver in 1967, Steve embarked on a legendary career that would span over six decades and touch nearly every sport sanctioned by the Colorado High School Activities Association (CHSAA). Steve’s officiating career began even earlier, at age 16, umpiring little league games. By 1962, while attending the University of Illinois on a football and track scholarship, he registered with the National Federation and began officiating intramurals—an effort to help pay for school while also honing his skills. His dedication to officiating only grew from there. Upon moving to Colorado, Steve joined the Aurora Sports Officials under the guidance of Dave and Mert Letofsky and was later welcomed into the Jefferson County Baseball Umpires’ Association. There, he worked alongside future CHSBUA Hall of Fame legends like Julius Carabello, Lou Nolin, Bud Schoepflin, and Joe Rossi. Despite limited on-field training in those days, Steve’s natural leadership and dedication quickly earned him championship-level assignments. Over the years, Steve would become known for his precision, professionalism, and unwavering commitment to the game. He has officiated multiple district, regional, and state finals—most notably a state championship baseball game in 2023 at the age of 79, where he served as the plate umpire. His remarkable estimate: over 10,000 baseball games officiated, and nearly 30,000 total across all sports. Outside of officiating, Steve’s career as a chemical engineer took him to Amoco Oil, where he led hazardous waste recovery projects and earned patents for innovative oil treatment technologies. During his assignment at the Rocky Mountain Arsenal, he received a direct commission to captain and oversaw critical operations. Today, Steve remains the senior member of CHSBUA and continues to umpire both baseball and softball. His encyclopedic knowledge of the rules has made him a respected authority—often the go-to person for resolving rule disputes both on and off the field. “It’s easier to just ask Steve than to search the rule book. He’s always right, and no one has ever disagreed with him. He’s the final word.” Steve Heuer isn’t just an umpire. He is a steward of the game, a mentor, and a living archive of the evolution of officiating in Colorado. His contributions have inspired generations—and continue to shape the CHSBUA today. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Steve Usecheck Jr.
wordpress.contentText: Steve Usecheck Jr. Class of 1999 Steve Usechek, Jr. joined the Rocky Mountain Umpires Association around 1968 and remained an active member until the formation of the CHSBUA in the early 1970s. He became a respected umpire throughout the Denver metropolitan area, working high school baseball in every major conference through 1984. In addition to his high school responsibilities, Steve umpired games for local colleges and universities, including conference assignments in the Western Athletic Conference and the Big Eight. Among his most meaningful achievements was officiating State Championship games across all classifications of high school baseball. Steve also served as assigning commissioner for the Skyline and Front Range high school leagues. He was known for providing opportunities to developing officials, helping many young umpires progress from junior varsity to varsity roles. Colleagues regarded him as a fair and supportive assignor who gave deserving officials a chance to establish themselves in high school baseball. Steve concluded his umpiring career in 1984 when he accepted positions on the basketball officiating staff of the Western Athletic Conference and the football officiating staff of the Big Eight. Over the next fifteen years he worked numerous postseason assignments, including the Freedom, Citrus, Copper, Las Vegas, and Sugar Bowls, and served as an alternate for the 1994 Rose Bowl. Steve and his wife, Florence, have been married for thirty‑four years and have three daughters and two granddaughters. He retired from the Boulder Valley School District in 1998 after a thirty‑one‑year career as a special education teacher and currently works at Indian Peaks and The Ranch Country Club golf facilities. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box
