wordpress.name: Terry Angell
wordpress.contentText: Terry Angell Class of 2026 - Morrison Terry Angell is a dedicated and influential figure in Colorado baseball umpiring, with a career spanning more than 29 years. His love of baseball began in youth leagues, but he paused his pursuits after marrying at 18, starting a family (two children, six grandchildren), and building a career in autobody repair. He later taught autobody repair for 24 years, yet baseball remained a constant passion. He coached his children in both baseball and softball, and once they were grown, umpiring kept him on the field and working with kids. Terry began by attending Robert Wilson’s clinic for JCJBL and then worked as many games as possible year‑round—so many that his wife, Laura, joked she became “a baseball widow.” Since starting high school games in 1996, Terry has been a fixture on the field with CHSBUA , working countless contests across nearly three decades. His reputation earned him five championship assignments and numerous semifinal and postseason games (2006 5A final, 2010 3A final, 2014 4A semifinal and final, 2019 5A semifinal and final, 2024 5A final). In addition to his local work, Terry attended multiple umpire clinics, including the highly regarded UTEP Clinic, which further refined his skills and rules knowledge. He trained and worked diligently to earn the sport’s most competitive assignments. Terry’s attention to detail is evident not only on the field but also in his administrative service—managing registration, taking on treasurer duties, and serving as CHSBUA webmaster, which earned him the nickname “registration guru.” Known for his extensive contributions to CHSBUA , Terry has worn many hats: Area 1 Director (AD), Secretary/Treasurer, and currently Treasurer (now in his 10th year). He has supported Area 1 New Umpire Training and the Pueblo Clinic; helped operate the Denver Broncos Araserve fundraiser booth; assisted with the Hall of Fame and five supporting golf tournaments; attended the NASO Conference; participated in Officiate Colorado Day; and worked the Coors Field Clinic in Denver. These accomplishments are featured on the CHSBUA website, which he has strengthened since 2016. He helped create job descriptions for all Board of Directors roles—including the Executive Committee and Area Directors—developed statewide umpire data spreadsheets for ADs, and produced a Treasurer’s Toolkit that documents responsibilities for smooth transitions in the future. Beyond administration, Terry remains deeply involved in the technical and teaching side of officiating. He trains newer umpires, shares his experience generously, and plays a pivotal role in developing the next generation of officials. Terry’s legacy is one of unwavering dedication, versatility, and commitment to excellence—both on and off the field. As he likes to say, he has done it all “for love of the game!” Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Terry Garvey
wordpress.contentText: Terry Garvey Class of 2015 Terry Garvey graduated in 1966 from Hinkley High School , where he played for the renowned coach Terry Schiessler. He went on to attend Mesa Junior College in Grand Junction. After completing his time at Mesa, Terry returned home hoping to find a coaching position with the Aurora American Legion program. But a former coach—and fellow umpire—Bob Finney had other plans and introduced Terry to umpiring youth baseball. That moment launched a career that would span nearly 40 years. Terry was fortunate to learn from several outstanding mentors. His early rules instruction came from Dave Letofsky, who spent countless hours at his kitchen table teaching Terry and other young umpires the finer points of the rulebook. It was Mert Letofsky who gave Terry his start in college baseball, assigning him to games in the RMAC in the early 1980s. Terry went on to work college baseball for the next 20 years, officiating in both the RMAC and the WAC. One of Terry’s favorite memories was a high‑scoring matchup between Grand Canyon University | Explore GCU's Degree Programs and Denver University , which ended somewhere around 46–12. He recalls the DU catcher driving in 13 runs on his own that day. Over the years, Terry worked numerous CHSAA State Playoffs in high school baseball. His summer baseball résumé is equally long and storied. He worked his first A‑Legion State Championship with Joe Rossi and Terry Schiessler, and he would go on to work many state and regional American Legion and AABC tournaments. Terry was also one of the original members of the early‑1980s “A‑Team,” a group that helped form the Colorado Collegiate Baseball Association . In 1984, he was selected to umpire the USA Olympic Team vs. Japan game held at Mile High Stadium . Terry and Keith Bailey share a rare distinction: they are the only two men who both played at Mile High (in the Denver Suburban All‑Star Game) as high school seniors and later umpired a game there. Terry also umpired in the MLB Legends Baseball Game. Throughout his career, Terry brought smiles to players, coaches, and partners across the Front Range. Nights spent working at Aurora Central, Littleton, Lowry Air Force Base, and Fitzsimmons Army Fields, and long days at Cherry Creek, Hinkley, and Gateway High School, remain among his most memorable experiences. Terry has been married to Donna for 46 years. They have two sons, Michael and Caz, and Terry is the proud grandfather of three grandchildren. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Terry Reed
wordpress.contentText: Terry Reed Class of 2025 Terry Reed has called thousands of games across decades — but his greatest victories came off the field. With a career spanning over 40 years, Terry Reed has become a pillar of Colorado’s officiating community. From district and regional matchups to the intensity of state championship games, Terry earned respect with every pitch, every call, and every show of quiet professionalism. His calm demeanor, sharp judgment, and deep knowledge of the game made him a trusted presence behind the plate and on the court. His impact was felt far beyond Colorado's borders.In 2018, Terry was invited to umpire at the prestigious Dreams Park Tournament in Cooperstown, NY — a personal and professional highlight that allowed him to visit the National Baseball Hall of Fame. He’s also been awarded bronze, silver, and gold medals from ASA/USA Softball, holds National Indicator Fraternity honors, and has received multiple Umpire of the Year awards. Born and raised in Kansas, Terry moved to Colorado in 1976. A player in both men’s fast pitch and slow pitch softball, he eventually found his true calling in officiating. Over the years, he became a versatile 3-sport official in baseball, softball, and basketball under CHSAA, and also served two terms as a softball area director. Off the field, he’s a proud husband to Penny (married since 1977), father to three daughters, and grandfather to six. “Terry’s character has always been his strongest trait,”says fellow official and longtime friend.“Even in the toughest moments, you could count on him to show up with strength, faith, and grace.” A Fight Bigger Than the Game In 2023, Terry faced an opponent tougher than any he’d seen on the field: cancer. After experiencing severe abdominal pain, doctors discovered a tumor in his hip and pelvis — bone cancer that had originated in his kidney. The diagnosis was dire. Terry stepped away from officiating, believing his time was short. CHSAA honored him at the 5A state championship with a ceremonial first pitch, surrounded by family, friends, and fellow officials. But in true Terry Reed fashion, he refused to quit.Thanks to an experimental surgery and an indomitable spirit, Terry defied the odds. By mid-2023, he was walking and jogging again. By 2024, he was back on the field — whistle in hand, smile on his face. Faith, Family, and the Future Terry credits his miraculous recovery to God, the unwavering love of his family, and the support of the officiating community. He continues to receive monthly treatments, with his cancer now in remission. Through it all, his perspective remains grounded in gratitude and hope. “I believe I’m here for a reason,” he says.“There’s more to give, more to teach, and more to live.” With every game he calls today, Terry Reed is a living testament to resilience, faith, and the enduring love of the game. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Terry Schiessler
wordpress.contentText: Terry Schiessler Class of Pre-1990 A fundamental pillar in Colorado baseball history, Terry Schiessler played an essential role in high school athletics from the early 1960s through the 1980s. Although passionate about baseball, he excelled as a three‑sport all‑state athlete in Nebraska. He came to Colorado and the University of Denver on a basketball scholarship but ultimately became a standout in both football and baseball. After graduation, he was drafted by the St. Louis Cardinals and Philadelphia Eagles, yet chose to begin his career in the Aurora Public Schools system in 1958. Terry launched his coaching career at Aurora Central High School as a junior varsity baseball coach before becoming the inaugural head coach at Aurora Hinkley High School. Starting with an entire roster of underclassmen, he built the program into a powerhouse, guiding the Thunderbirds to three consecutive Centennial League championships and State Tournament berths while compiling a remarkable 72–26 record. In 1968, he earned National District 7 High School Coach of the Year honors and managed the Suburban All‑Stars in the annual Denver‑Suburban game. He later completed his master’s degree and became the first head coach at Metropolitan State College. Terry’s career then shifted toward administration, where he served as athletic director at Aurora Central and Northglenn High School. His coaching influence extended beyond Colorado, managing in the Basin League in South Dakota from 1967–74, where he coached future professionals such as John Stearns, Paul Splitorff and Bob Apodaca. He also spent the 1975 season coaching the Arvada Dons. A respected multi‑sport official, Terry worked football, basketball and baseball at both the high school and college levels. He officiated numerous State Championship games and was a familiar presence on the sidelines as part of the Denver Broncos’ “chain gang.” His college baseball officiating included several years at the National Junior College Baseball World Series in Grand Junction. Terry also led the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association as president from 1977–1979 and served on executive boards for basketball and football officials. Throughout his distinguished career, Terry Schiessler earned induction into numerous halls of fame and left a lasting legacy on Colorado athletics through his leadership, integrity and unwavering dedication to young athletes. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box Hall of Fame Inductee Terry Schiessler – Aurora A fundamental pillar in Colorado baseball history, Terry Schiessler was integrally involved in all aspects of high school athletics from the early 1960s through the 80s. His passion was baseball. A three-sport all-state star from Nebraska, Terry came to Colorado and the University of Denver on a basketball scholarship but ended up being a standout in both football and baseball. Upon graduation, he was drafted by both the St. Louis Cardinals and Philadelphia Eagles, but chose to begin his career in the Aurora Public Schools system in 1958. After starting his baseball coaching career at Aurora Central as a junior varsity coach, Terry became the inaugural head coach at Aurora Hinkley. After starting with all underclassmen, he led the Thunderbirds to three consecutive Centennial League championships and state playoff berths with a 72-26 record. In 1968, he was named the National District 7 High School Coach of the Year and managed the Suburban All-Stars in the annual Denver-Suburban game. Terry took time to complete his master’s degree and became the first head coach at Metropolitan State College. Terry’s focus turned to administration where he became the athletic director at Aurora Central and Northglenn high schools. He also served as manager in the Basin League in South Dakota from 1967- 74. He coached players such as John Stearns, Paul Splitorff and Bob Apodaca. In 1975, he coached the Arvada Dons. Terry also was a top-notch football, basketball and baseball official and was a fixture on the sidelines while working the “chain gang” for the Denver Broncos. He officiated state finals in all three high school sports and moved into the college level where he worked several years at the National Junior College Baseball World Series in Grand Junction. He also was the president of the Colorado High School Baseball Umpires Association from 1977 – 1979 and served on executive boards in basketball and football. He has been inducted in myriad hall of fames.

wordpress.name: Thomas Severtson
wordpress.contentText: Thomas Severtson Class of 2009 Thomas “Sev” W. Severtson, a Denver native born on May 30, 1948, has devoted his life to education, coaching, and the development of Colorado’s youth. A graduate of East High School, he continued his academic journey at the University of Northern Colorado, earning his MA in 1971. Sev and his wife, Belinda, make their home in Denver. Sev’s distinguished career in education led him to serve as the Dean of Students at Rangeview High School, a role he has regarded as both a responsibility and an honor. His commitment to students was matched by an exceptional coaching career that set a standard for excellence in Colorado baseball. In 1994, Sev received two of the most prestigious coaching honors of his career: Colorado Baseball Coach of the Year and the AABC District Seven Coach of the Year Award. Over the years, he was also named Denver Public Schools Coach of the Year six times, a testament to his consistency, leadership, and integrity. With an extraordinary record of 338 wins and 100 losses, Sev achieved a level of success and recognition rarely seen in high school athletics. His expertise made him a sought‑after speaker and clinician at several State and Local Baseball Clinics, where he shared his knowledge with coaches across the region. Known for his character as much as his accomplishments, Sev’s integrity remains a defining hallmark of his legacy. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Tim Kardatzke
wordpress.contentText: Tim Kardatzke Class of 2017 Tim Kardatzke umpired his first baseball game in 1976 while living in Toledo, Ohio. In 1983, he and his wife, Jan, moved t o Broomfield, Colorado, where he began working CHSAA baseball. Over the next 39 years, Tim became a reliable and respected official, spending most of his career on full varsity assignments before retiring in 2015. In addition to baseball, Tim has officiated volleyball since 1977 and continues to remain active in that sport. He has also been a longtime Leader in the Boy Scouts of America . A public‑school educator since 1971, Tim continues to serve Colorado students today as an active substitute teacher. Throughout his CHSBUA career, Tim was a significant contributor to Area 10 (Boulder/Longmont), where he worked two CHSAA State Championships and approximately 15 Regional assignments. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Tom Freed
wordpress.contentText: Tom Freed Class of 1996 Tom Freed began his umpiring career in 1960 and remained active through 1984. He devoted many of his early years to coaching Little League and umpiring in Las Animas and surrounding southeastern Colorado communities. In those days, umpires contacted high schools directly for assignments, earning $10 to $15 per game with no mileage. They frequently lined fields, set bases, and prepared game sites themselves. Freed joined the Lamar Umpire Association in 1964 and the La Junta Umpires in 1971. He worked numerous regional, district, and state tournaments, and also officiated baseball for Otero Junior College and Trinidad Junior College. His commitment to the craft was recognized throughout the region, where he became known for his professionalism and consistency on the field. Alongside his officiating career, Freed worked at the VA Hospital in Fort Lyons until retiring in 1980. He later served as a substitute teacher and administrative assistant at Las Animas High School, further strengthening his ties to the community. Freed has also served as municipal judge in Las Animas since 1973, contributing more than five decades of dedicated public service to the city he proudly represents. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Tom Robinson
wordpress.contentText: Tom Robinson Class of 2024 “Beyond the impact Tom Robinson has made in this Association, our state, and nationally over the course of his 30-plus year career as a coach, teacher, administrator, white-hat referee, Hall of Famer, Big 12 replay expert, husband, father, and grandfather, we are all better people for having had Tom in our lives,” said CHSAA commissioner Rhonda Blanford-Green. Hired by former commissioner Bob Ottewill, Tom became the first African-American administrator in the history of CHSAA. Over his 20 years with the Association, Tom oversaw officials — becoming the first to assign a female official to a boys’ basketball Final Four — as well as the sportsmanship program and boys’ and girls’ golf. A role model and respected leader, he was widely admired for his passion, integrity, and mentorship. “Tom was an inspiration in so many ways,” said CHSAA assistant commissioner Bethany Brookens. “He taught me how to play golf, he and Cleo taught us all about love and marriage, he taught me the mechanics of officiating, and he gave me loyal friendship and a deep respect for all people. An incredible, ethical, good man and mentor — I already miss him dearly.” A graduate of Regis Jesuit High School, Tom attended Colorado State University, where he competed in football, basketball, and track and field. He began officiating in the summer of 1969, and that same year started his teaching and coaching career at Regis Jesuit. He remained there for 31 years, serving as head basketball coach from 1983 to 1993, before joining CHSAA in 2001. Tom and his wife, Cleo Parker Robinson, were married for 51 years. Tom stood as a steadfast presence behind the scenes of Cleo’s dream: the formation and rise of the nationally recognized Cleo Parker Robinson Dance (CPRD). As a co-founder, board member, and business manager, he contributed to every facet of the organization’s success. Beyond CHSAA, Tom served as president of the National Association of Sports Officials and worked as the Big 12 Conference’s replay coordinator. He announced his retirement on December 15, 2021. Tom’s passing leaves an enduring void, but his legacy of compassion, leadership, and service lives on. “Even from the outside looking in, Tom was a person to aspire to be,” said Rashaan Davis, CHSAA’s first assistant commissioner for activities. “He always seemed cool, calm, and collected — something I know I needed. Now, as a staff member, I understand that Tom laid the foundation for me, a Black male in this role, to be patient and to recognize all voices. Nobody did it better.” Tom is survived by his wife, Cleo Parker Robinson; his son, Malik Robinson, executive director of CPRD; and his beloved grandchildren. He looked forward to playing golf with Cleo and being present in his grandchildren’s lives. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Tom Smith
wordpress.contentText: Tom Smith Class of 2013 Tom “Tozzi” Smith was born in Flint, Michigan, and now resides in Superior, Colorado, with his wife, Hilary. They are the proud parents of two children: Alannah Rose Blaze and Jenna Hilary Smith. Tom attended St. Michael’s High School in Flint, where he earned seven varsity letters—two in football as a quarterback, three in baseball as a catcher, and two in basketball as a guard. He graduated from St. Bernard in Cullman, Alabama, in 1966 with a Bachelor’s degree in English Literature, where he also played two years of collegiate baseball. He later pursued graduate studies at Eastern Michigan University in Physical Education, earning an M.S. in Health and Recreation, and completed post‑graduate work at the University of Colorado, receiving a Type D Administration Certificate. Tom’s educational and coaching career began at Father Lopez High School in Daytona Beach, Florida, where he taught English and served as Head Baseball Coach, Assistant Football Coach, and Assistant Basketball Coach for three years. He then moved to Boulder High School, teaching English and coaching baseball, basketball, and swimming—serving as Head Swimming Coach for four years. Tom later joined Fairview High School, where he spent 26 years as an English teacher (including time as Department Chair), JV Baseball Coach, Dean of Students, and Athletic/Activities Director. Most recently, Tom has served at Holy Family High School as Dean of Students, Assistant Principal, and Athletic Director. Tom’s officiating career spanned 40 years (1972–2012). During that time, he worked four State Championship Finals and six Semi‑Finals in baseball. He served as President of CHSBUA from 1995 to 1997 and received the Ken Furman Memorial Award in 2004. Tom also spent 25 years as a softball umpire—working four Finals and four Semi‑Finals—and officiated basketball for 25 years, with multiple Finals and Semi‑Finals assignments. He has served as Secretary/Treasurer since 2011. Tom has been honored with numerous distinctions, including induction into the Michigan High School Athletic Association Hall of Fame, the Michigan High School Football Coaches Hall of Fame, and Charter Membership in the Flint Sports Hall of Fame. Although accomplished in many areas, Tom’s greatest passion has always been officiating. What began as a way to provide extra income for his family became a 40‑year vocation defined by challenge, professionalism, and pride. He expanded his officiating work to include basketball and later softball, routinely rushing from a full day of teaching or administration to officiate an evening game. He took immense pride in providing a high‑quality service to student‑athletes and coaches, feeling the satisfaction of excellence just as deeply as the sting of a missed call. Even in retirement, Tom admits he misses the adrenaline of calling big games. With a long history of postseason assignments, it’s clear his peers and assignors recognized his skill, judgment, and commitment. The greatest influence in Tom’s life was his father, Tom Smith Sr.—a legendary coach and teacher at Flint St. Michael’s for 38 years. Tom speaks often of the values and principles instilled by his parents, who were married for 63 years and passed away within just 11 days of each other. Their lasting impact continues to guide his life and career. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Virgil Jester
wordpress.contentText: Virgil Jester Class of 1989 1989 Hall of Fame Inductee Virgil Jester – Westminster Virgil Jester came out of Denver North High School and became a fixture in Denver baseball. After attending Colorado State College of Education, he played three seasons with the Denver Bears and played in the major leagues with the Braves, first in Boston and then Milwaukee. He started as an infielder but was quickly moved to the pitching mound because his fastball reached speeds into the 90 mph range. Jester signed a contract with the Boston Braves in 1947 for $2,500. As he looked back on his career a few years ago, he said he was born 55 years too soon, missing the big salaries that came to major-league baseball. Jester pitched in the old Denver Post tournament at Merchants Park on South Broadway. He once pitched against Hall of Famer Satchel Paige. In his 21-game MLB career, he posted a 3–5 record and a 3.84 career earns run average, permitting 84 hits and 27 bases on balls in 75 career innings pitched; he was credited with 25 strikeouts. Jester retired from baseball after the 1959 season. He was back with the Bears for his final year. Virgil Jester, a 1989 Westminster Hall of Fame inductee, emerged from Denver North High School ) and became a prominent figure in Denver baseball. After attending the Colorado State College of Education—known today as the University of Northern Colorado —he went on to play three seasons with the Denver Bears ) before reaching Major League Baseball with the Braves, first in Boston and later in Milwaukee. Jester began his career as an infielder but was quickly moved to the mound due to a fastball that reached the 90‑mph range. In 1947, he signed with the Boston Braves for $2,500, a franchise that later relocated and became the Milwaukee Braves , for whom he also appeared at the major‑league level. Throughout his playing years, Jester competed in the historic Denver Post Tournament at Merchants Park , a prominent Denver baseball venue known for attracting barnstorming greats. During one memorable appearance, he pitched against legendary Hall of Famer Satchel Paige . Over the course of his 21-game Major League career, Jester recorded a 3–5 win–loss record, a 3.84 ERA, 84 hits allowed, 27 walks, 25 strikeouts, and 75 innings pitched. He retired from professional baseball after the 1959 season, closing out his career with one final year back home with the Denver Bears. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Walt Clay
wordpress.contentText: Walt Clay Class of 1990 Walt Clay earned a reputation as one of the toughest competitors of his era, remembered vividly by former coach, official, and radio personality Irv Brown, who once called him “the toughest s.o.b. I ever saw.” His rugged style gained national attention in 1946, when a Chicago sportswriter praised the talented backfield of the Chicago Rockets as “a dream” and described Clay—their right halfback—as the “nightmare.” Standing six‑foot‑one and weighing 228 pounds, the former United States Marine Corps athlete brought extraordinary strength and intensity to professional football. Clay first made his mark at Longmont High School, helping lead the team to the 1940 state co‑championship. Playing through a separated shoulder held in place with a horse harness, he powered across the goal line in the closing moments to tie the title game—a performance that became part of Colorado sports history. He continued his ascent at the University of Colorado, earning first‑team All‑Big Seven honors as a freshman fullback in 1942 before leaving college to join the Marines during World War II. After his military service, Clay returned to the field in the All‑America Football League, competing for both Chicago and Los Angeles until the league folded in 1949. A versatile athlete, he also played guard for the AAU Denver American Legion basketball team, which reached the 1943 national finals. He later completed his degree at CU in 1951 and transitioned into coaching. Clay continued to shape Colorado athletics at Centennial High School in Pueblo, where he coached football and basketball for nine years. He then embarked on a long and respected officiating career, working basketball and wrestling for twenty‑eight years, football for twenty‑five, and baseball and softball for twenty. His toughness, professionalism, and dedication left a lasting impact across generations of athletes and officials. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Warren Kettner
wordpress.contentText: Warren Kettner Class of 1990 Warren Kettner, a 1990 Hall of Fame inductee, was born in the farming community of Good Thunder, Minnesota ( City of Good Thunder ). His early education began at St. Paul’s Lutheran Elementary School in Denver ( Saint Paul Lutheran Community of Faith ) and continued at Truman High School in Minnesota (Truman Public Schools). He later attended Lower Columbia College in Longview, Washington ( Lower Columbia College ). After serving two years in the U.S. Army in South Korea from 1954 to 1955, he completed his history degree at Valparaiso University in Indiana ( Valparaiso University ). In 1959, he began teaching and coaching at Lutheran High School in Denver, then known as Denver Lutheran and now part of Lutheran High School Parker ( Lutheran High School Parker ). There he taught geography, world history, international relations, and economics while leading the physical education department. He founded the school’s cross‑country and wrestling programs and directed its sports medicine program for 35 years. He also served as an assistant coach in basketball and football and spent ten years as athletic director. In baseball, where he left his greatest mark, he became head coach in 1963 and spent 25 years guiding the program, compiling a record of 213–156, winning six Metro League championships, reaching four state semifinals, earning Metro League Coach of the Year in 1980, and securing a District Coach of the Year nomination in 1985. He also contributed to the state baseball committee and worked with notable figures such as Willie White and Del Peterson, ultimately earning recognition as a Metro League Hall of Fame member. wordpress.name: William "Bill" Fanning
wordpress.contentText: William "Bill" Fanning Class of 1991 William “Bill” Fanning stands as a legendary figure in Western Slope athletics, leaving a lasting mark as a coach, official, and community voice throughout his decades-long career in Colorado high school sports. Bill arrived in Grand Junction in 1954 to play for the Grand Junction Eagles semipro baseball team. Soon after, he joined Grand Junction High School as a teacher and head baseball coach. His coaching tenure produced remarkable success, including three state championships and six state runner-up finishes. From 1956–1995, Bill also built a distinguished officiating career across multiple sports. He worked baseball, basketball, football, and softball throughout the Western Slope, earning widespread respect for his consistency and professionalism. In 1958, he umpired the inaugural National Junior College World Series in Grand Junction and continued working the event for fifteen consecutive years—longer than any official from that first crew. Following his years on the field and court, Bill became a familiar presence on local airwaves, serving as a radio announcer with KREX for more than a decade. Bill’s impact on student‑athletes and high school sports has been honored through induction into the Colorado High School Athletic American Hall of Fame, the Colorado Coaches Sports Hall of Fame, and the National High School Sports Hall of Fame. His legacy endures at Grand Junction High School, where the practice field proudly bears his name. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box

wordpress.name: Willie White
wordpress.contentText: Willie White Class of 1991 Willie White holds the unique distinction of earning a World Series ring with the Philadelphia Phillies as a longtime member of their scouting staff. While this achievement reflects his deep knowledge of the game, Willie’s influence on baseball extends across many levels and decades. From 1970–1990, Willie served as head baseball coach at Littleton High School, guiding his teams to two state championships. He also coached throughout the summers, helping develop countless young athletes. In recognition of his impact, the baseball field at Littleton High School now bears his name. A charter member of the CHSBCA and the Dugout Club, Willie played a central role in organizing the Colorado High School All State Baseball games during the many years they were held at Englewood High School. He also contributed his expertise on numerous district, state, and local selection committees. Willie’s coaching honors include being named Mr. Baseball by CHSAA in 1990 and CHSAA Coach of the Year in 1991. He is also a member of the South High School Hall of Fame and an honorary member of the Colorado Baseball Umpires Association (CHSBUA). Willie is married to Margaret and is the father of three daughters and two sons, with nine grandchildren who continue the family’s proud baseball legacy. Share This Story Blocksy: Share Box
